Also, the last sentence is translated in much better style in the first translation, a good example of avoiding the too literal when necessary.

source: Ein Schweigen folgte. Es wartete jeder auf eine 脙聞u脙聼erung des anderen. Der einzelne hatte sich wohl in den letzten Tagen gepr脙录ft, wohin, zu wem seine Gedanken gingen; doch bleibt die R脙录ckkunft Verstorbener, das hei脙聼t: die W脙录nschbarkeit solcher Wiederkehr immer ein verwickeltes und heikles Ding. Im Grunde und gerade heraus gesprochen besteht sie nicht, diese W脙录nschbarkeit; sie ist ein Irrtum; sie ist, bei Lichte besehen, genau so unm脙露glich, wie die Sache selbst, was sich erweisen w脙录rde, h脙露be die Natur die Unm脙露glichkeit dieser nur einmal auf; und was wir Trauer nennen, ist vielleicht nicht sowohl der Schmerz 脙录ber die Unm脙露glichkeit, unsere Toten ins Leben kehren zu sehen, als dar脙录ber, dies gar nicht w脙录nschen zu k脙露nnen.
translation_a: Silence followed. Everyone was waiting for someone else to say something. Over the last few days, each of them had probably scrutinized the direction of his own thoughts, asked himself to whom they reached out. And yet, the return of those who have died芒聙聰or better, the desirability of such a return芒聙聰is always a complicated, ticklish matter. Ultimately, to put it plainly, it does not exist, this desirability. It is a miscalculation; by the light of cold day, it is as impossible as the thing itself, which would be immediately evident if nature rescinded that impossibility even once; and what we call mourning is perhaps not so much the pain of the impossibility of ever seeing the dead return to life, as the pain of not being able to wish it.
translation_b: A silence followed. Everyone waited for a statement from the other. In the last few days, the individual had probably checked where his thoughts went, to whom; but the return of the deceased, that is, the desirability of such a return, always remains a complicated and delicate matter. Basically, and to put it bluntly, this desirability does not exist; she is a mistake; viewed in the light of day, it is just as impossible as the thing itself, which would be proved if nature abolished its impossibility just once; and what we call sorrow is perhaps not so much the grief at the impossibility of seeing our dead come to life as at not being able to wish it at all.
comment: The wording of the second translation if often awkward, too literal. There is also a wrong pronoun. The first translation shows a much more elegant style, for example "Over the last few days, each of them had probably scrutinized the direction of his own thoughts" sounds far better than "In the last few days, the individual had probably checked where his thoughts went, to whom", it is an overall much better read.

source: Er bat Herrn Doktor Fuchstaler, die Ambulanz zu leiten, und mu脙聼te sich gestehen, da脙聼 er lieber hier geblieben als auf den Galitzinberg gefahren w脙陇re. Und doch, es mu脙聼te sein. Nicht nur sich allein gegen脙录ber war er verpflichtet, der Sache weiter nachzugehen; noch allerlei anderes gab es heute zu erledigen. Und so entschlo脙聼 er sich f脙录r alle F脙陇lle, Herrn Doktor Fuchstaler auch mit der Abendvisite zu betrauen. Das junge M脙陇dchen mit dem verd脙陇chtigen Spitzenkatarrh dort im letzten Bett l脙陇chelte ihm zu. Es war dieselbe, die neulich bei Gelegenheit einer Untersuchung ihre Br脙录ste so zutraulich an seine Wange gepre脙聼t hatte. Fridolin erwiderte ihren Blick ungn脙陇dig und wandte sich stirnrunzelnd ab. Eine wie die andere, dachte er mit Bitterkeit, und Albertine ist wie sie alle 芒聙?sie ist die Schlimmste von allen. Ich werde mich von ihr trennen. Es kann nie wieder gut werden.
translation_a: He asked Doctor Fuchstaler to run the ambulance and had to admit that he would rather have stayed here than gone to the Galitzinberg. And yet it had to be. He was under an obligation not only to himself to pursue the matter further; there were a lot of other things to do today. And so he decided, just in case, to entrust Doctor Fuchstaler with the evening rounds as well. The young girl with the suspicious catarrh there in the last bed smiled at him. It was the same one who had pressed her breasts so trustingly against his cheek the other day during an examination. Fridolin returned her look ungraciously and turned away frowning. One like the other, he thought bitterly, and Albertine is like them all - she is the worst of them all. I will break up with her. It can never be good again.
translation_b: He asked Dr Fuchstaler to take charge of the rounds, though he had to admit that he would rather have stayed on than drive out to the Galitzinberg. And yet it had to be done. He did not just owe it to himself to pursue the matter further; and there were many things still to be attended to today. And so, just in case, he decided to entrust Dr Fuchstaler with the evening rounds as well. The young girl with suspected acute bronchitis over in the last bed smiled at him. She was the one who during a recent examination had taken the opportunity to press her breast so intimately against his cheek. Fridolin returned her gaze ungraciously and turned away with a frown. They芒聙聶re all the same, he thought bitterly, and Albertine no different from the rest 芒聙?in fact she芒聙聶s the worst of them all. We芒聙聶ll have to part. Things can never be the same again between us.
comment: The wording of the second translaton is much better and smoother. It also fits the original style and time period of the book better, for example when using "We'll have to part" instead of the too modern "I will break up with her" in the first translation.

source: Und langsamen, z脙露gernden Schritts nahm er den Weg durch die wohlbekannten H脙露fe nach dem Pathologisch-anatomischen Institut. Er fand das Tor unverschlossen, so da脙聼 er nicht n脙露tig hatte zu klingeln. Der steinerne Fu脙聼boden hallte unter seinen Tritten, als er durch den schwach beleuchteten Gang schritt. Ein vertrauter, gewisserma脙聼en heimatlicher Geruch von allerlei Chemikalien, der den angestammten Duft dieses Geb脙陇udes 脙录bert脙露nte, umfing Fridolin. Er klopfte an die T脙录r des histologischen Kabinetts, wo er wohl noch einen Assistenten bei der Arbeit vermuten durfte. Auf ein etwas unwirsches 脗禄Herein脗芦 trat Fridolin in den hohen, geradezu festlich erhellten Raum, in dessen Mitte, das Auge eben vom Mikroskop entfernend, wie Fridolin beinahe erwartet, sein alter Studienkollege, der Assistent des Institutes, Doktor Adler, sich von seinem Stuhl erhob.
translation_a: With slow, hesitant steps he made his way across the courtyards to the Pathology Institute. He found the main gate unlocked, so he did not have to ring. The stone floor echoed beneath his feet as he walked along the dimly lit passage. A familiar, almost domestic smell of various chemicals, which drowned out the native odour of the building, enveloped Fridolin. He knocked at the door of the histology lab, where he suspected there might be a technician still at work. On hearing a rather irritable 芒聙聵Come in芒聙? he entered the high-ceilinged, almost festively illuminated room, in the middle of which, as Fridolin had half expected, his old fellow student, the Institute technician Dr Adler had just withdrawn his eye from a microscope, and was now rising from his chair.
translation_b: And with slow, hesitant steps he made his way through the well-known courtyards to the pathological-anatomical institute. He found the gate unlocked so he didn't need to ring the bell. The stone floor rumbled under his feet as he strode down the dimly lit corridor. A familiar, almost homely smell of all sorts of chemicals that drowned out the ancestral scent of this building enveloped Fridolin. He knocked on the door of the histology cabinet, where he probably still suspected an assistant at work. At a somewhat rude "Come in," Fridolin stepped into the high, almost festively lit room, in the middle of which, removing his eye from the microscope, as Fridolin almost expected, his old fellow student, the institute's assistant, Doctor Adler, rose from his chair he lifted.
comment: The second translations contains several mistakes, including quite big ones, such as "the institute's assistant, Doctor Adler, rose from his chair he lifted" or the rumbling stone floor. The first translation avoids such mistakes, uses better wording and is a much better read overall.

source: So ging es mit Wiedemann und Sonnenschein. Alle, die es sahen, bebten noch stundenlang. Es ist vergleichsweise eine Wohltat, im Gegensatz zu solcher Misere von einem wahren Ehrenhandel zu erz脙陇hlen, der ebenfalls dieser Periode angeh脙露rt, und der seinen Namen allerdings, der formalen Feierlichkeit wegen, mit der er gehandhabt wurde, bis zur L脙陇cherlichkeit verdiente. Hans Castorp wohnte ihm in seinen einzelnen Phasen nicht bei, sondern belehrte sich 脙录ber den verwickelten und dramatischen Hergang nur an der Hand von Dokumenten, Erkl脙陇rungen und Protokollen, die, diese Sache betreffend, im Hause Berghof und au脙聼erhalb seiner, n脙陇mlich nicht nur am Ort, im Kanton, im Lande, sondern auch im Auslande und in Amerika abschriftlich vertrieben und auch solchen zum Studium zugestellt wurden, von denen ohne weiteres sicher sein mu脙聼te, da脙聼 sie der Angelegenheit auch nicht einen Deut von Teilnahme widmen konnten und wollten.
translation_a: That was the Wiedemann-Sonnenschein affair. Everyone who witnessed it was still quivering hours later. In contrast to such misery, it is a comparative pleasure to tell about a genuine 芒聙聹affair of honor芒聙?that belongs to this same period and that definitely deserves the name, absurdly so, given the solemn formality with which it was carried out. Hans Castorp was not present at its various phases, but only learned about the complicated and dramatic course of events from documents, affidavits, and official minutes devoted to the affair, copies of which were circulated not only in the Berghof, not only in Davos, in the canton of Graub脙录nden, in Switzerland, but also abroad, were sent as far as America, and were made available for study to persons who, one can be sure, would not and could not pay one whit of attention to the matter.
translation_b: That's how it went with Wiedemann and sunshine. All who saw it continued to tremble for hours. It is comparatively a blessing, in contrast to such misery, to tell of a true bargain of honour, which also belongs to this period and which certainly deserved its name to the point of ridicule because of the formal solemnity with which it was handled. Hans Castorp did not attend its individual phases, but informed himself about the complicated and dramatic course of events only by means of documents, declarations and protocols, which, concerning this matter, were kept in the Berghof house and outside of him, namely not only at the place copies were distributed in the canton, in the country, but also abroad and in America, and were also sent to those for study who had to be sure that they could not and did not want to devote the least bit of interest to the matter.
comment: The second translation is awkwardly worded and contains some mistakes. It is also too literal. The first translation reads smoother, has better wording and focuses on the sense, not the literal translation.

source: Wie so die J脙陇hrchen wechselten, begann etwas umzugehen im Hause Berghof, ein Geist, dessen unmittelbare Abstammung von dem D脙陇mon, dessen b脙露sartigen Namen wir genannt haben, Hans Castorp ahnte. Mit der unverantwortlichen Neugier des Bildungsreisenden hatte er diesen D脙陇mon studiert, ja, bedenkliche M脙露glichkeiten in sich vorgefunden, an dem ungeheuerlichen Dienste, den die Mitwelt ihm widmete, ausgiebig teilzunehmen. Dem Wesen zu fr脙露nen, das jetzt um sich griff, nachdem es 脙录brigens, genau wie das alte, keimweise und da und dort sich andeutend schon immer vorhanden gewesen, war er nach seiner Gem脙录tsart wenig geschaffen. Trotzdem bemerkte er mit Schrecken, da脙聼 auch er, sobald er sich ein wenig gehen lie脙聼, in Miene, Wort und Gehaben einer Infektion unterlag, der niemand in der Runde sich entzog.
translation_a: As the years passed, something began to happen in the Berghof house, a spirit whose direct descent from the demon whose malicious name we have given Hans Castorp suspected. He had studied this demon with the irresponsible curiosity of an educational traveller, indeed found dubious possibilities within himself to participate extensively in the outrageous service that his contemporaries devoted to him. Indulging in the being that was now spreading, after it, like the old one, had always existed as a germ and hinted at it here and there, he was little suited to his disposition. Nevertheless, he noticed with horror that as soon as he let himself go a little, his face, speech and behavior succumbed to an infection from which no one in the group escaped.
translation_b: And as one little year succeeded another, a ghost began to walk the Berghof, one whom Hans Castorp suspected was a direct descendent of that demon we have already called by its malicious name. With the irresponsible curiosity of the tourist thirsting for knowledge, he had studied that old demon, indeed had found potentialities within himself for lively participation in the monstrous acts of homage paid to it by the world all about him. Like the old demon, this new one had always been around, sprouting up here and there to hint at its presence, but as it spread now, it became clear that by temperament Hans Castorp was less suited for worship of this creature. All the same, the moment he let himself go the least little bit, he noted to his horror that his words, gestures, and expression, too, had succumbed to an infection no one in the place could escape.
comment: The second translation starts of with a mistake ("little year") but apart from that is much better than the second one. Again, the first translation sticks too close to the words of the original, less to the meaning, which makes some sentences hard to read and occasionally even changes the meaning. The second translation captures the sense and style much better by not clinging to the exact words of the original.

source: Aus Neugierde eilte K. noch zur T脙录r, er wollte sehen, wohin die Frau getragen wurde, der Student w脙录rde sie doch nicht etwa 脙录ber die Stra脙聼en auf dem Arm tragen. Es zeigte sich, da脙聼 der Weg viel k脙录rzer war. Gleich gegen脙录ber der Wohnung f脙录hrte eine schmale h脙露lzerne Treppe wahrscheinlich zum Dachboden, sie machte eine Wendung, so da脙聼 man ihr Ende nicht sah. 脙聹ber diese Treppe trug der Student die Frau hinauf, schon sehr langsam und st脙露hnend, denn er war durch das bisherige Laufen geschw脙陇cht. Die Frau gr脙录脙聼te mit der Hand zu K. hinunter und suchte durch Auf- und Abziehen der Schultern zu zeigen, da脙聼 sie an der Entf脙录hrung unschuldig sei, viel Bedauern lag aber in dieser Bewegung nicht. K. sah sie ausdruckslos wie eine Fremde an, er wollte weder verraten, da脙聼 er entt脙陇uscht war, noch auch, da脙聼 er die Entt脙陇uschung leicht 脙录berwinden k脙露nne.
translation_a: Out of curiosity, K. hurried to the door, he wanted to see where the woman was being carried, the student wouldn't be carrying her across the street in his arms. It turned out that the way was much shorter. Directly opposite the apartment, a narrow wooden staircase probably led to the attic, it turned so that you couldn't see the end. The student carried the woman up these stairs, very slowly and groaning, because he was weak from walking. The woman waved her hand down at K. and tried to show by shrugging her shoulders that she was innocent of the kidnapping, but there wasn't much regret in this gesture. K. looked at her blankly, like a stranger, he didn't want to reveal that he was disappointed, nor that he could easily overcome his disappointment.
translation_b: Out of curiosity, K. hurried to the door, wanting to see where the woman was being taken; the student surely wouldn芒聙聶t carry her through the streets in his arms. It turned out the path was much shorter. Directly across from the apartment door a narrow flight of wooden stairs led upward, probably to an attic area; they made a turn, so you couldn芒聙聶t see where they ended. The student was carrying the woman up these stairs, very slowly now, and groaning, for he was weakened by his previous efforts. The woman waved down at K., and tried to show by a shrug of her shoulders that the abduction wasn芒聙聶t her fault, but there wasn芒聙聶t a great deal of regret in the gesture. K. looked at her without expression, like a stranger, wishing to show neither his disappointment, nor that he could easily overcome it.
comment: Both translations contain slight errors, leaving out some words which change the original meaning somewhat. The second translation makes less mistakes (for example it contains the necessary "surely" in the first sentences which the first translation leaves out) and also has smoother wording (such as "wanting to see" instead of "he wanted to see"), so that it makes for better reading overall.

source: In Verlegenheit oder Ungeduld r脙录ckte der Untersuchungsrichter auf seinem Sessel hin und her. Der Mann hinter ihm, mit dem er sich schon fr脙录her unterhalten hatte, beugte sich wieder zu ihm, sei es, um ihm im allgemeinen Mut zuzusprechen oder um ihm einen besonderen Rat zu geben. Unten unterhielten sich die Leute leise, aber lebhaft. Die zwei Parteien, die fr脙录her so entgegengesetzte Meinungen gehabt zu haben schienen, vermischten sich, einzelne Leute zeigten mit dem Finger auf K., andere auf den Untersuchungsrichter. Der neblige Dunst im Zimmer war 脙陇u脙聼erst l脙陇stig, er verhinderte sogar eine genauere Beobachtung der Fernerstehenden. Besonders f脙录r die Galeriebesucher mu脙聼te er st脙露rend sein, sie waren gezwungen, allerdings unter scheuen Seitenblicken nach dem Untersuchungsrichter, leise Fragen an die Versammlungsteilnehmer zu stellen, um sich n脙陇her zu unterrichten. Die Antworten wurden im Schutz der vorgehaltenen H脙陇nde ebenso leise gegeben.
translation_a: Embarrassed or impatient, the coroner shifted in his chair. The man behind him, with whom he had spoken before, leaned toward him again, either to give him general encouragement or to give him some specific advice. Downstairs people were talking quietly but animatedly. The two parties, who previously seemed to have had such opposite opinions, mingled, some people pointed their fingers at K., others at the examining magistrate. The foggy haze in the room was extremely annoying, it even prevented a closer observation of those who were far away. It must have been particularly annoying for the visitors to the gallery, who were forced to ask quiet questions to the participants in order to get more information, albeit with shy sidelong glances at the examining magistrate. The answers were given just as quietly, protected by the hands held in front of them.
translation_b: The examining magistrate shifted about in his chair in embarrassment or impatience. The man behind him, with whom he had been talking earlier, bent down to him again, either to give him some general words of encouragement or to pass on special advice. The people below conversed quietly but animatedly. The two parties, which had appeared to hold such contrasting opinions before, mingled with one another, some people pointing their fingers at K., others at the examining magistrate. The foglike haze in the room was extremely annoying, even preventing any closer observation of those standing further away. It must have been particularly disturbing for the visitors in the gallery, who were forced, with timid side glances at the examining magistrate of course, to address questions under their breath to the members of the assembly in order to find out what was happening. The answers were returned equally softly, shielded behind cupped hands.
comment: The first translation at first uses the word "coroner", which is not a good choice and then later suddenly switches to the more correct term "examining magstrate". While it uses better wording than the second translations occasionally ("leaned toward" vs. "bent down to" or just "mingle" vs. "mingled with one another", the second translation reads smoother altogether, which is especially noticeable in the last two sentences.

source: Bequem und nicht ohne W脙录rde trug er auf seinen Schultern die hohe Zivilisation, welche die herrschende Oberschicht der handeltreibenden Stadtdemokratie ihren Kindern vererbt. Er {52}war so gut gebadet wie ein Baby und lie脙聼 sich von jenem Schneider kleiden, der das Vertrauen der jungen Leute seiner Sph脙陇re besa脙聼. Der kleine, sorgf脙陇ltig gezeichnete W脙陇scheschatz, den die englischen Z脙录ge seines Schrankes bargen, ward von Schalleen aufs beste betreut; noch als Hans Castorp ausw脙陇rts studierte, schickte er ihn regelm脙陇脙聼ig zur Reinigung und Ausbesserung nach Hause (denn seine Maxime war, da脙聼 man au脙聼er in Hamburg im Reiche nicht zu b脙录geln verstehe), und eine aufgerauhte Stelle an der Manschette eines seiner h脙录bschen farbigen Hemden h脙陇tte ihn mit heftigem Unbehagen erf脙录llt. Seine H脙陇nde, obgleich nicht sonderlich aristokratisch in der Form, waren gepflegt und frisch von Haut, mit einem Kettenring aus Platin und dem gro脙聼v脙陇terlichen Erbsiegelring geschm脙录ckt, und seine Z脙陇hne, die etwas weich waren und mehrfach Schaden gelitten hatten, mit Gold erg脙陇nzt.
translation_a: Comfortably and not without dignity, he carried on his shoulders the high civilization which the ruling upper class of the commercial urban democracy inherited from their children. He was bathed as well as a baby and had himself clothed by that tailor who had the confidence of the young people of his sphere. The small, carefully drawn treasure of linen, which the English features of his closet contained, was well looked after by Schalleen; Even when Hans Castorp was studying abroad, he regularly sent him home to be cleaned and repaired (for his maxim was that except in Hamburg one did not know how to iron in the Reich), and a rough spot on the cuff of one of his pretty colored shirts would have him filled with intense uneasiness. His hands, though not particularly aristocratic in form, were trim and fresh of skin, adorned with a chain ring of platinum and the grandfather's hereditary signet ring, and his teeth, which were somewhat soft and had been damaged several times, were supplemented with gold.
translation_b: Comfortably, not without dignity, he carried the weight of culture with which the governing upper class of the commercial city endowed its children. He was as clean as a well-cared-for baby, and dressed by the tailor in whom the young men of his social sphere felt most confidence. Schalleen took beautiful care of his small stock of carefully marked linen, which was bestowed in a dressing-chest on the English plan. When he studied away from home, he regularly sent back his laundry to be washed and mended, for it was a saying of his that outside Hamburg nobody in the kingdom knew how to iron. A rough spot on the cuff of his dainty coloured shirts filled him with acute discomfort. His hands, though not particularly aristocratic in shape, were well tended and fresh-skinned, and he wore a platinum chain ring as well as the seal ring inherited from Grandfather. His teeth were rather soft and defective and he had a number of gold fillings.
comment: The second translation flows much better. It is less literal and uses more sophisticated wording and sentence structure.

source: Eins aber bereitete ihm Genugtuung, wenn er lag und auf sein Herz, sein k脙露rperliches Herz achtete, das rasch und vernehmlich in der Stille pochte, 芒聙?der vorschriftsm脙陇脙聼igen Hausordnungsstille, die w脙陇hrend der Haupt- und Schlafliegekur 脙录ber dem ganzen 脗禄Berghof脗芦 waltete. Es pochte hartn脙陇ckig und vordringlich, sein Herz, wie es das fast best脙陇ndig tat, seitdem er hier oben war; doch nahm Hans Castorp neuerdings weniger Ansto脙聼 daran als in den ersten Tagen. Man konnte jetzt nicht mehr sagen, da脙聼 es auf eigene Hand, grundlos und ohne Zusammenhang mit der Seele klopfte. Ein solcher Zusammenhang war vorhanden oder doch unschwer herzustellen; eine rechtfertigende Gem脙录tsbewegung lie脙聼 sich der exaltierten K脙露rpert脙陇tigkeit zwanglos unterlegen. Hans Castorp brauchte nur an Frau Chauchat zu denken 芒聙?und er dachte an sie 芒聙? so besa脙聼 er zum Herzklopfen das zugeh脙露rige Gef脙录hl.
translation_a: One thing, however, gave him satisfaction when he lay and paid attention to his heart, his physical heart, which was beating quickly and audibly in the silence - the prescribed house rules silence that reigned over the entire "Berghof" during the main and sleep rest cure. It was beating persistently and urgently, his heart, as it had almost constantly since he was up here; but recently Hans Castorp took less offense than in the first days. One could no longer say that it was knocking on one's own hand, for no reason and without any connection with one's soul. Such a connection existed or was at least not difficult to establish; a justifying emotional emotion could be casually added to the exalted physical activity. Hans Castorp only had to think of Mrs. Chauchat - and he thought of her - and he had the feeling to make his heart pound.
translation_b: He did take satisfaction in one thing, however, as he lay there listening to his heart, his physical heart, pounding rapidly and audibly in the stillness芒聙聰the stillness that was prescribed by house rules and reigned over the entire Berghof during the main rest cure of the day. His heart was pounding insistently, urgently, the way it had done almost constantly ever since he had arrived here; and yet of late that did not upset Hans Castorp as it had the first few days. One could no longer say that it thudded on its own accord, for no reason, and without any connection to his soul. There was a connection now, or at least it would not have been difficult to establish one芒聙聰a justifiable emotion could easily be assigned to his body芒聙聶s overwrought activity. Hans Castorp needed only to think of Frau Chauchat芒聙聰and he did think of her芒聙聰and his heart had a suitable emotion to make it pound.
comment: The second translation reads much smoother and more elegant. Both translations work comparatively well, but the second one is more polished and omits superfluous words without losing the sense of the original.

source: Man lachte, und da der Humanist auffahren wollte, erz脙陇hlte Hans Castorp rasch von Schl脙陇gen, die er selbst einst empfangen: auf seinem Gymnasium war in den unteren Klassen diese Strafe teilweise noch get脙陇tigt worden, es waren Reetst脙露cke vorhanden gewesen, und wenn auch die Lehrer an ihn nicht Hand hatten legen m脙露gen, gesellschaftlicher R脙录cksicht halber, so war er doch von einem st脙陇rkeren Mitsch脙录ler einmal gepr脙录gelt worden, einem gro脙聼en Flegel, mit dem biegsamen Stock auf die Oberschenkel und die nur mit Str脙录mpfen bekleideten Waden, und das hatte ganz schm脙陇hlich weh getan, infam, unverge脙聼lich, geradezu mystisch, unter sch脙陇ndlich innigem Sto脙聼schluchzen waren ihm die Tr脙陇nen nur so hervorgest脙录rzt vor Wut und ehrlosem Wehsal 芒聙?Herr Wehsal mochte freundlichst das Wort entschuldigen 芒聙? und Hans Castorp hatte denn auch gelesen, da脙聼 in Zuchth脙陇usern bei Empfang der Pr脙录gelstrafe die st脙陇rksten Raubm脙露rder wie kleine Kinder flennten.
translation_a: Everybody laughed; but while the humanist was bringing up his guns, Hans Castorp hastily began to talk about a beating he had once received, when he was in one of the lower forms in the gymnasium, where this form of punishment still survived to some extent, and there were always switches on hand. His, Hans Castorp芒聙聶s social position had been too good for the masters to venture to lay hands on him; but he had once been whipped by a stronger pupil, a big lout of a fellow who had laid on with the flexible switch across Hans Castorp芒聙聶s thin-stockinged calves. It had hurt so confoundedly芒聙?so 芒聙聹mystically芒聙聺芒聙聰that he had fairly sobbed for rage, and the tears had ignominiously flowed down. And he recalled having read that in the penitentiaries, when men are flogged, the most hardened reprobates will blubber like little children.
translation_b: People laughed, and since the humanist wanted to burst out, Hans Castorp quickly told of the beatings he himself had once received: this punishment had sometimes still been imposed in the lower classes at his grammar school, there had been thatched sticks, even if the teachers were on had not wanted to lay hands on him, out of social consideration, he had been beaten by a stronger classmate, a big lout, with a flexible stick on his thighs and his calves, which were only clad in stockings, and that had hurt terribly, infamous, unforgettable, downright mystical, with shameful, heartfelt sobs, his tears had spurted out of anger and dishonorable misery - Mr. Wehsal would kindly excuse the word - and Hans Castorp had also read that in prisons the strongest are when they receive beatings Murderers cried like little children.
comment: The first translation shows a better understanding for the meaning of the original text and reads smoother. The second text contains actual mistakes ("even if the teachers were on had not wanted to lay hands on him" - this part makes no sense, or "murderers" written with a capital M) and uses less convincing wording. Also, the first translation - rightly - leaves out the wordplay with the German word "Wehsal" and the name "Wehsal" as this can't be translated well, whereas the second translation translates it and disregards the fact that it makes no sense this way.

source: Das war ersch脙录tternd in jedem Sinn; Hans Castorp war begeistert von der Begegnung, und zugleich sp脙录rte er etwas wie aufsteigende Angst, eine Beklemmung derselben Art, wie das Eingesperrtsein mit dem g脙录nstigen Ungef脙陇hr auf engem Raum ihm verursachte: auch dies, da脙聼 der l脙陇ngst vergessene Pribislav ihm hier oben als Frau Chauchat wieder begegnete und ihn mit Kirgisenaugen ansah, war wie ein Eingesperrtsein mit Unumg脙陇nglichem oder Unentrinnbarem, 芒聙?in begl脙录ckendem und 脙陇ngstlichem Sinn Unentrinnbarem. Es war hoffnungsreich und zugleich auch unheimlich, ja bedrohlich, und ein Gef脙录hl der Hilfsbed脙录rftigkeit kam den jungen Hans Castorp an, 芒聙?in seinem Inneren vollzogen sich unbestimmte und instinktm脙陇脙聼ige Bewegungen, die man als ein Sichumsehen, als ein Tasten und Suchen nach Hilfe, nach Rat und St脙录tze h脙陇tte ansprechen m脙露gen; er dachte nacheinander an verschiedene Personen, an die zu denken etwa zutr脙陇glich sein mochte.
translation_a: It was thrilling in every sense of the word. Hans Castorp was inspired by this meeting, and at the same time he sensed something like a growing anxiety, much like that suffocating feeling of being locked up in a box together with auspicious chance; what was more, the fact that the long-forgotten Pribislav had reappeared up here as Frau Chauchat and had looked at him with Kirghiz eyes made him feel as if he were locked up together with something inevitable and inescapable芒聙聰an inescapability that both cheered and alarmed him. translation_b: Lovingly he gazed into the flowing water, into the transparent green, into the crystal lines of its mysterious patterning. translation_a: Pangloss replied as follows: 芒聙聰My dear Candide! you knew Paquette, that pretty maidservant to our august Baroness. In her arms I tasted the delights of paradise, which directly caused these torments of hell, from which I am now suffering. She was infected with the disease, and has perhaps died of it. Paquette received this present from an erudite Franciscan, who took the pains to trace it back to its source; for he had it from an elderly countess, who picked it up from a captain of cavalry, who acquired it from a marquise, who caught it from a page, who had received it from a Jesuit, who during his novitiate got it directly from one of the companions of Christopher Columbus.15 As for me, I shall not give it to anyone, for I am a dying man.
translation_b: Pangloss replied in these terms: 芒聙聹O my dear Candide! you knew Paquette, that pretty servant of our august baroness; I tasted in his arms the delights of paradise, which produced those torments of hell with which you see me devoured; she was infected with it, she may have died of it. As for me, I won't give it to anyone, because I'm dying.

translation_a: I promised a dialogue, I keep my word. 芒聙?It was morning at daybreak: the rays of the sun gilded both the summit of Mount Viso and that of the highest mountains of the island which is at our antipodes; and she was already awake, either because her premature awakening was the effect of the nocturnal visions which often put her in an agitation as tiring as it was useless, or because the carnival, which was then drawing towards its end, was the occult cause of her awakening. , this time of pleasure and madness having an influence on the human machine like the phases of the moon and the conjunction of certain planets. 芒聙聹Finally, she was awake, and wide awake, when my soul rid itself of the bonds of sleep.

translation_a: Simpson's situation was getting worse; the symptoms of approaching death could not be overlooked by the doctor. This one could do nothing about it; he suffered cruelly himself from a painful ophthalmia which could lead to blindness, if he was not careful. The twilight then gave a sufficient quantity of light, and this light, reflected by the snows, burned the eyes; it was difficult to protect against this reflection, because the lenses of the glasses, becoming covered with an icy crust, became opaque and intercepted the view. However, it was necessary to watch carefully for the slightest accidents on the road and to find them as far as possible; it was therefore necessary to brave the dangers of ophthalmia; however, the doctor and Bell, covering their eyes, left it to each of them in turn to steer the sled.
translation_b: Simpson芒聙聶s condition was getting worse; Clawbonny could not mistake the signs of approaching death. The doctor could do nothing; he was suffering cruelly himself from a painful snow-blindness which might lead to loss of sight if he was not careful. At that time the half-light was sufficient, and this light, reflected by the snow, burned his eyes; it was difficult to protect himself from the reflection, for the lenses of his glasses often got covered with ice and so became opaque, stopping him seeing. But they had to avoid the slightest accidents on the journey, preventing them as much as possible; they were therefore obliged to run the risk of snow-blindness; the doctor and Bell took turns leading the sledge and covering their eyes.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. I didn't find any major error in the "not so good" translation, but some parts are really awkward-sounding in English. For example, in French, we have an expression "celui-ci" (literally, "this one") that we often use to refer to someone just mentioned in the context. In this text, we find that expression in "Celui-ci n芒聙聶y pouvait rien". Translating that expression directly into English would be strange because that is not something that is used in English. Yet, that's what the "not so good" translation did in: "This one could do nothing about it". It is a tricky thing to translate, but I think that the good translation did a great job by choosing to repeat the NP (noun phrase) associated to the person that "celui-ci" refers to in "The doctor could do nothing".

He had stayed with his face turned towards the curtains, following, on the transparence of the cloth, the pale morning light, the blaze of noon, the mauve of twilight, all the colours, all the emotions of the heavens. Upon that screen were reflected even the quivering of the warm air when a bird flaps its wings, and the joyfulness of the scents throbbing within a sunbeam. Behind that veil, that softened dream of the powerful life outside, he could hear spring arriving. And at times he was even suffocated, when in spite of the barrier of the curtains the rush of the earth芒聙聶s new blood came at him too brutally.
translation_b: But the next day he was seized with the fear of a child. He never allowed the windows to be wide open. He whispered, 芒聙聹Soon, later. He remained anxious, he was worried about the first flash of light he would receive in his eyes. The evening arrived, that he had not been able to make the decision to see the sun again in front. He had remained with his face turned towards the curtains, following on the transparency of the linen the pale morning, the ardent noon, the violet twilight, all the colors, all the emotions of the sky. There, even the thrill that the flapping of a bird's wings gives to the warm air, even the joy of smells, quivering in a ray. Behind this veil, behind this tender dream of the powerful life outside, he listened to spring rising. And he even suffocated a little, at times, when the influx of new blood from the earth, despite the obstacle of the curtains, came to him too harshly.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other one is too literal and some of the sentences don't make sense in English. Take the French sentence "[...] la d脙漏cision de revoir le soleil en face". The expression "revoir ... en face" means, word by word, "to see again ... facing" which would normally be translated as "to face again". The GOOD translation got it right in "[...] the decision to face the sun again" while the BAD translation has it as "[...] the decision to see the sun again in front" which makes no sense in English.

translation_a: He had broken the law of 19 Vent脙麓se year XI, article I, which prohibits any individual without a diploma from practicing medicine; so that, on dark denunciations, Homais had been summoned to Rouen, near the king's attorney, to his private office. The pharmacist's ears made him think he was going to fall suddenly; he caught a glimpse of the pit bottoms, his family in tears, the pharmacy sold, all the jars scattered; and he was obliged to go into a cafe to have a glass of rum with seltzer water, to recover his spirits.
translation_b: He had broken the law of 19 Vent脙麓se, Year XI, Article 1, which forbids any individual not possessing a diploma to practice medicine; with the consequence that, on the basis of mysterious denunciations, Homais had been summoned to appear in Rouen, before the king芒聙聶s prosecutor, in his private chambers. comment: The translation I selected is much better as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other translation is more literal. I did not find serious errors or mistranslations in the "not so good" translation. It just sticks to French syntax too much. The good translation made an effort to use different sentence structure and vocabulary to really convey the meaning in this story. When you stay too close to French syntax, the story doesn't make a lot of sense in English.

source: Ils se pla脙搂aient dans la salle basse d芒聙聶un cabaret, qui avait 脙 sa porte des filets noirs suspendus. Ils mangeaient de la friture d芒聙聶脙漏perlans, de la cr脙篓me et des cerises. Ce n芒聙聶脙漏tait pas la premi脙篓re fois qu芒聙聶ils apercevaient des arbres, du ciel bleu, du gazon, qu芒聙聶ils entendaient l芒聙聶eau couler et la brise soufflant dans le feuillage ; mais ils n芒聙聶avaient sans doute jamais admir脙漏 tout cela, comme si la nature n芒聙聶existait pas auparavant, ou qu芒聙聶elle n芒聙聶e脙禄t commenc脙漏 脙 脙陋tre belle que depuis l芒聙聶assouvissance de leurs d脙漏sirs.
translation_a: They placed themselves in the lower room of a cabaret, which had black nets hanging from its door. They ate fried smelts, cream and cherries. They lay down on the grass; they kissed apart under the poplars; and they would have liked, like two Robinsons, to live perpetually in this little place, which seemed to them, in their bliss, the most magnificent on earth. It was not the first time they had seen trees, blue skies, grass, heard the water running and the breeze blowing through the foliage; but they had doubtless never admired all that, as if nature had not existed before, or had only begun to be beautiful since the satisfaction of their desires.
translation_b: They would sit in the low-ceilinged room of a tavern with black nets hanging at its door. They would eat fried smelts, cream, and cherries. They would lie down in the grass; they would go off and kiss under the poplars; and they would wish that, like two Robinson Crusoes, they could live forever in that little spot, which seemed to them, in their bliss, the most magnificent on earth. It was not the first time they had seen trees, blue sky, or lawn, or that they had heard water trickling and the breeze blowing through the leaves; but they had never before admired all of this, it was as if nature had not existed before, or as if it had begun to be beautiful only once they had slaked their desires.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other translation is too literal and contains a few errors. For example, the bad translation has the wrong tense on its verbs. The French tense used throughout the text is the Imperfect, used here to describe a habitual action in the past. All the actions mentioned are actions that the characters did a number of times over a certain period of time. In English, it is appropriate to translate this idea with "they used to" or "they would +V-infinitive". Ex: Ils mangeaient = They used to eat / They would eat. // The bad translation chose to use the preterit (i.e., they ate) throughout the text, which makes it sounds like each action only happened once.

Des bouts de jardin, conquis sur le roc, 脙漏talaient des carr脙漏s de l脙漏gumes, coup脙漏s de haies vives. 脙聙 cette heure, les Artaud 脙漏taient vides ; pas une femme aux fen脙陋tres, pas un enfant vautr脙漏 dans la poussi脙篓re ; seules, des bandes de poules allaient et venaient, fouillant la paille, qu脙陋tant jusqu芒聙聶au seuil des maisons, dont les portes laiss脙漏es ouvertes b脙垄illaient complaisamment au soleil. Un grand chien noir, assis sur son derri脙篓re, 脙 l芒聙聶entr脙漏e du village, semblait le garder.
translation_a: The priest, his eyes dazzled, lowered his gaze to the village, whose few houses were scattering below the church. Wretched houses, made of dry stones and masonry planks, thrown along a narrow path, with no marked streets. They were about thirty in number, some heaped up in the manure, black with misery, others larger, more cheerful, with their pink tiles. Pieces of garden, reclaimed from the rock, displayed squares of vegetables, cut with quickset hedges. At this hour, the Artauds were empty; not a woman at the windows, not a child wallowing in the dust; only flocks of hens came and went, rummaging in the straw, begging to the thresholds of the houses, whose doors, left open, gaped complacently in the sun. A large black dog, sitting on his behind, at the entrance to the village, seemed to guard him.
translation_b: The priest now turned his dazzled eyes down to the village, with its few houses scattered haphazardly below the church. Wretched houses, made of drystone walls and planks, scattered along a narrow path with no indication of streets. There were about thirty of them, some piled up over a dungheap and black with poverty, others bigger and brighter with pink roof tiles. The bits of garden, won from the rock, showed plots of vegetables, divided by hedges. At this hour, Les Artaud was empty, with no woman at the windows, no child sprawling in the dust; just bands of chickens going around, fossicking through the straw, searching for food right up to the thresholds of the houses, whose open doors yawned pleasantly in the sun. A big black dog, sitting on its haunches at the entry to the village, seemed to be guarding it.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other translation is too literal, and contains a few errors. For example, the direct object pronoun "le" (=him/it depending on the antecedent) on the last line refers to "the village". Therefore it must be translated as "it" since the village is inanimate. The good translation correctly has it as "it". The bad translation incorrectly has it as "him" which makes the text confusing because it seems that all of a sudden there is a man in the picture, when in reality there isn't.

translation_a: 芒聙聵Yes, suddenly you understand it, you don芒聙聶t know why nor in response to what, and then everything in life looks different. In my case, for the past fifteen years I芒聙聶ve felt it wearing me down like some creature gnawing away at my being. It芒聙聶s taken from me my firm skin, my muscles, my teeth, the whole of my former body, leaving me only my despairing soul which will soon be taken from me too.
translation_b: Yes, you suddenly understand it, you don't know why or about what, and then everything changes in life. For fifteen years, I have felt it working on me as if I I carried within me a gnawing animal. I felt it little by little, month by month, hour by hour, degrading me like a house that is collapsing. It disfigured me so completely that I do not recognize myself. I have nothing left of me, of me the radiant, fresh and strong man that I was at the age of thirty. I saw her dye my black hair white, and with what skillful and malicious slowness! took my firm skin, my muscles, my teeth, my whole body of old, leaving me only a desperate soul that she will soon take away too.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other translation is way too literal. Also, the bad translation misinterpreted the verb "travailler" here. While "travailler" usually means "to work" - here it is used as an idiomatic expression that means "to make someone worry" / "to be on somebody's mind". The good translation correctly got it as: "I芒聙聶ve felt it wearing me down" while the bad translation has it as: "I have felt it working on me" which doesn't mind anything in English.

translation_a: Serge lived there alone, in painful anxiety, more suffocating, sleeping on the sofa, trying to escape the sighs of the park, the smells of old furniture. At night, the nudity of the paintings gave him mad dreams, of which he only kept a nervous anxiety when he woke up. He thought he was ill again; his health had one last need to recover completely, the need for a supreme plenitude, for an entire satisfaction which he did not know where to go to seek. So he passed his days, silent, his eyes bruised, waking with a slight start only at the hours when Albine came to see him. They remained facing each other, looking at each other gravely, with rare, very sweet words, which saddened them. Albine's eyes were even more bruised than Serge's, and they implored him.
translation_b: Serge lived there on his own, in a state of painful anxiety, suffocating even more, and sleeping on the sofa to try to avoid the sighs rising from the park and the odours of the old furniture. At night the naked figures in the paintings gave him mad dreams, of which all that remained when he awoke was a nervous uneasiness. He decided he had fallen ill again; his health needed one more thing to be fully recovered; that need was for a supreme fullness of being, an absolute satisfaction, and he did not know where it might be sought. So he spent the days in silence, with dark-ringed eyes, only coming awake with a slight start when Albine came to see him. They would stand, facing each other, with just a few tender words that made them even more unhappy. Albine芒聙聶s eyes were even darker than Serge芒聙聶s, and seemed to beseech him.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best. The other one is too literal and contains awkward phrasing. For example, compare the GOOD translation: "Serge lived there [...] suffocating even more" with the BAD translation: "Serge lived there [...] more suffocating". The part "more suffocating" sounds very weird in English. Additionally, I believe that some of the French was mistranslated. Take the French "Les yeux d芒聙聶Albine 脙漏taient encore plus meurtris [...]" -- This clearly means that her eyes are showing signs of depression or something like that, and not that there is physical damage to her eyes. The GOOD translation has it as: "Albine芒聙聶s eyes were even darker [...]" while the BAD translation has it as: "Albine's eyes were even more bruised [...]" I feel like this is saying that she got beaten up and got bruised eyes as a result. Even if you can use "bruised" metaphorically to mean "depressed", that would make the bad translation ambiguous. No need to add ambiguity when there isn't any in French.

source: Le jour revint, et il fut bien constat脙漏 que, sous l芒聙聶influence d芒聙聶un courant sous-marin, le banc de glace d脙漏rivait vers le nord avec rapidit脙漏. Cette masse flottante emportait le Forward, clou脙漏 au milieu de l芒聙聶ice-field, dont on ne voyait pas la limite ; dans la pr脙漏vision d芒聙聶une catastrophe, dans le cas o脙鹿 le brick serait jet脙漏 sur une c脙麓te ou 脙漏cras脙漏 par la pression des glaces, Hatteras fit monter sur le pont une grande quantit脙漏 de provisions, les effets de campement, les v脙陋tements et les couvertures de l芒聙聶脙漏quipage ; 脙 l芒聙聶exemple de ce que fit le capitaine MacClure dans une circonstance semblable, il fit entourer le b脙垄timent d芒聙聶une ceinture de hamacs gonfl脙漏s d芒聙聶air de mani脙篓re 脙 le pr脙漏munir contre les grosses avaries ; bient脙麓t la glace, s芒聙聶accumulant sous l芒聙聶influence d芒聙聶une temp脙漏rature de sept degr脙漏s (-14脗掳 centigrades) ; le navire fut entour脙漏 d芒聙聶une muraille de laquelle sa m脙垄ture sortait seule.
translation_a: The day returned, and it was clearly observed that, under the influence of an undercurrent, the ice bank was drifting rapidly towards the north. This floating mass carried the Forward, nailed to the middle of the ice-field, the limit of which could not be seen; in anticipation of a catastrophe, in case the brig should be thrown on a slope or crushed by the pressure of the ice, Hatteras brought on deck a large quantity of provisions, the camping effects, the clothes and blankets of the crew ; Following the example of what Captain MacClure did in a similar circumstance, he had the vessel surrounded by a belt of hammocks inflated with air so as to protect it against serious damage; soon the ice, accumulating under the influence of a temperature of seven degrees (-14脗掳 centigrade); the ship was surrounded by a wall from which its mast alone emerged.
translation_b: Day broke, and it was observed that the ice-field was floating north, in a strong submarine current. This floating mass transported the Forward, nailed to the heart of the field, without visible limit; foreseeing catastrophe if the brig was dashed on the coast or crushed by the ice, a large quantity of provisions, camping equipment, and blankets for the crew were brought on deck; like Captain McClure in similar circumstances, Hatteras had the ship protected with a ring of hammocks inflated with air, to prevent the worst damage; with soon a temperature of seven (芒聢?4脗掳C) and a build-up of ice, the ship was surrounded by a high wall, only its mast emerging.
comment: This was a difficult decision. For some reason, I am not a big fan of either translation. Both include great as well as poor phrasing. Ultimately, the reason why I chose the second translation is because the first one contains an error. In French, "[...] Hatteras fit monter sur le pont une grande quantit脙漏 de provisions [...]" means that Hatteras HAD SOMEONE do something. He didn't do this thing himself. The translation I selected uses an avoidance strategy: "[...] a large quantity of provisions [...] were brought on deck" where the use of the passive erases agentivity (the person or thing performing the action of the verb). That works, because even though it is not a very accurate translation, it does not contradict the original meaning. The translation I did NOT select, however, did go into this contradiction with: "[...] Hatteras brought on deck a large quantity of provisions [...]". This sentence is a contradiction to the original text and it is false. It might seem like a detail, but it could potentially be of crucial importance for the story. You never know.

source: Le Forward, dans la journ脙漏e du 16 juin, rangea le cap Aworth. Le mont Rawlinson dressait ses pics blancs vers le ciel ; la neige et la brume le faisaient para脙庐tre colossal en exag脙漏rant sa distance ; la temp脙漏rature se maintenait 脙 quelques degr脙漏s au-dessus de glace ; des cascades et des cataractes improvis脙漏es se d脙漏veloppaient sur les flancs de la montagne ; les avalanches se pr脙漏cipitaient avec une d脙漏tonation semblable aux d脙漏charges continues de la grosse artillerie. Les glaciers, 脙漏tal脙漏s en longues nappes blanches, projetaient une immense r脙漏verb脙漏ration dans l芒聙聶espace. La nature bor脙漏ale aux prises avec le d脙漏gel offrait aux yeux un splendide spectacle. Le brick rasait la c脙麓te de fort pr脙篓s ; on apercevait sur quelques rocs abrit脙漏s de rares bruy脙篓res dont les fleurs ros脙漏es sortaient timidement entre les neiges, des lichens maigres d芒聙聶une couleur rouge脙垄tre, et les pousses d芒聙聶une esp脙篓ce de saule nain, qui rampaient sur le sol.
translation_a: The Forward, during the day of June 16, ranged Cape Aworth. Mount Rawlinson raised its white peaks to the sky; the snow and mist made it look colossal by exaggerating its distance; the temperature was kept a few degrees above ice; waterfalls and improvised cataracts developed on the sides of the mountain; the avalanches rushed in with a detonation similar to the continuous discharges of heavy artillery. The glaciers, spread out in long white sheets, projected an immense reverberation in space. The boreal nature struggling with the thaw offered a splendid spectacle to the eyes. The brig skimmed the coast very closely; On some sheltered rocks one could see rare heathers whose pink flowers emerged timidly between the snows, scanty lichens of a reddish color, and the shoots of a species of dwarf willow, which crawled on the ground.
translation_b: On 16 June the Forward rounded Cape Acworth. Mount Rawlinson thrust its white peaks heavenwards; by exaggerating the distance, the snow and mist made it seem colossal; the temperature stayed a few degrees above that of ice; waterfalls and improvised cascades proliferated on the mountainside; avalanches rushed down, sounding like the continuous rumbling of heavy artillery. Glaciers, spread out in long white sheets, produced glaring reflections. Northern nature in the grip of a thaw was a wonderful sight. The brig hugged the coast; on a few sheltered rocks occasional heather was spotted, with pink flowers timidly emerging through the snow, together with thin reddish lichens and shoots of a species of dwarf willow crawling along the ground.
comment: The decision here was quite difficult to make. Both translations are fine. I think the one I selected is slightly better as it is a bit less literal than the other one. The very first line and the very last line in the other one are quite literal - sticking to French syntax too much. Example: NOT SO GOOD --> "The Forward, during the day of June 16, ranged Cape Aworth" vs. GOOD --> "On 16 June the Forward rounded Cape Acworth."

translation_a: They recognized the house by an old walnut tree which shaded it. Low and covered with brown tiles, it had outside, under the dormer window of its attic, a string of onions suspended. Bourr脙漏es, standing against the thorn fence, surrounded a patch of lettuce, a few vines of lavender, and flowerpots mounted on oars. Bundles of brushwood, leaning against the thorn hedge, surrounded a bed of lettuces, a few stalks of lavender, and some sweet peas tied up on sticks. Dirty water trickled out over the grass, and all around were odds and ends of tattered old garments, knitted stockings, a red calico wrapper, and a large, coarse linen sheet spread lengthwise on the hedge. At the sound of the gate, the wet nurse appeared, holding on her arm a nursing child. With her other hand, she was pulling along behind her a poor, sickly little boy whose face was covered with scrofulous sores, the son of a Rouen knit-goods dealer, left here in the country by his parents, who were too occupied with their business.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other translation is quite bad. One French word was left untranslated (i.e., bourr脙漏es) even though "bourr脙漏es" has a translation in English and is not a French word that is commonly used in English - the way that "d脙漏j脙 vu" is used in English, for example.

source: Et on discuta sur le cas d'adult脙篓re compliqu脙漏 de chantage. On n'en parlait point comme on parle, au sein des familles, des 脙漏v脙漏nements racont脙漏s dans les feuilles publiques, mais comme on parle d'une maladie entre m脙漏decins ou de l脙漏gumes entre fruitiers. On ne s'indignait pas, on ne s'脙漏tonnait pas des faits; on en cherchait les causes profondes, secr脙篓tes, avec une curiosit脙漏 professionnelle et une indiff脙漏rence absolue pour le crime lui-m脙陋me. On t脙垄chait d'expliquer nettement les origines des actions, de d脙漏terminer tous les ph脙漏nom脙篓nes c脙漏r脙漏braux dont 脙漏tait n脙漏 le drame, r脙漏sultat scientifique d'un 脙漏tat d'esprit particulier. Les femmes aussi se passionnaient 脙 cette poursuite, 脙 ce travail. Et d'autres 脙漏v脙漏nements r脙漏cents furent examin脙漏s, comment脙漏s, tourn脙漏s sous toutes leurs faces, pes脙漏s 脙 leur valeur, avec ce coup d'oeil pratique et cette mani脙篓re de voir sp脙漏ciale des marchands de nouvelles, des d脙漏bitants de com脙漏die humaine 脙 la ligne, comme on examine, comme on retourne et comme on p脙篓se, chez les commer脙搂ants, les objets qu'on va livrer au public.
translation_a: And they discussed this case of adultery complicated by blackmail. They did not talk about it the way you might comment, around a family dinner-table, on events reported in the press, but rather the way doctors talk about diseases, or greengrocers about vegetables. They showed neither indignation nor astonishment over the facts; they searched for their deep-seated, hidden causes, with a professional curiosity and a complete lack of interest in the crime itself. They tried to find clear explanations in terms of underlying motive, trying to identify all the mental phenomena behind the tragedy, seeing it as the scientific consequence of a particular state of mind. The women, too, found this investigation, this task, deeply engrossing. And other recent events were analysed, commented upon, explored from every point of view, weighed up precisely, with that practised eye and that specialized approach of the dealer in news, the vendor of the human comedy by the line, just as a tradesman examines, scrutinizes, and weighs up the products he is going to sell to the public.
translation_b: And we discussed the case of adultery complicated with blackmail. We didn't talk about it as we talk, within families, about the events recounted in the public newspapers, but as we talk about an illness between doctors or vegetables between fruit trees. We were not indignant, we were not surprised by the facts; the deep, secret causes were sought with professional curiosity and absolute indifference to the crime itself. We tried to explain clearly the origins of the actions, to determine all the cerebral phenomena from which the drama was born, the scientific result of a particular state of mind. Women too were passionate about this pursuit, this work. And other recent events were examined, commented on, shot from all sides, weighed at their value, with that practical eye and that special way of seeing news dealers, peddlers of human comedy on the line, like we examine, as we return and as we weigh, at the merchants, the objects which we are going to deliver to the public.
comment: This French text has a very interesting particularity: it uses the subject pronoun "on" (syntactically third person singular but semantically first person plural) with the meaning of "other people" (which is not normally the meaning assigned to "on"). Here, the original author is talking about a group of people from whom he is detached. This French "on" should thus definitely be translated as "they" - which is what the translation that I selected correctly does. The other translation kept the literal / usual meaning of "on" as "we" - which adds a serious error. In addition, the translation I selected is more idiomatic and flows well in English, contrary to the other one.

source: La journ脙漏e fut longue, le lendemain ! Elle se promena dans son jardinet, passant et revenant par les m脙陋mes all脙漏es, s芒聙聶arr脙陋tant devant les plates-bandes, devant l芒聙聶espalier, devant le cur脙漏 de pl脙垄tre, consid脙漏rant avec 脙漏bahissement toutes ces choses d芒聙聶autrefois qu芒聙聶elle connaissait si bien. Comme le bal d脙漏j脙 lui semblait loin ! Qui donc 脙漏cartait, 脙 tant de distance, le matin d芒聙聶avant-hier et le soir d芒聙聶aujourd芒聙聶hui ? Son voyage 脙 la Vaubyessard avait fait un trou dans sa vie, 脙 la mani脙篓re de ces grandes crevasses qu芒聙聶un orage, en une seule nuit, creuse quelquefois dans les montagnes. Elle se r脙漏signa pourtant ; elle serra pieusement dans la commode sa belle toilette et jusqu芒聙聶脙?ses souliers de satin, dont la semelle s芒聙聶脙漏tait jaunie 脙 la cire glissante du parquet. Son c脜聯ur 脙漏tait comme eux : au frottement de la richesse, il s芒聙聶脙漏tait plac脙漏 dessus quelque chose qui ne s芒聙聶effacerait pas.
translation_a: It was a long day the next day! She walked in her garden, passing and returning by the same alleys, stopping in front of the flowerbeds, in front of the espalier, in front of the plaster priest, contemplating with amazement all these things of the past that she knew so well. . How far off the ball already seemed to him! Who, then, kept aside, at such a distance, the morning of the day before yesterday and the evening of today? His trip to Vaubyessard had made a hole in his life, like those great crevasses which a storm, in a single night, sometimes digs in the mountains. She resigned herself, however; she piously tucked away her beautiful toilette and even her satin shoes, the soles of which had become yellow from the slippery wax of the parquet floor, in the chest of drawers. His heart was like them: in the friction of wealth, something had been placed upon it that would not be erased.
translation_b: How long the next day was! She walked in her little garden, going and coming along the same paths, stopping in front of the flower beds, the espaliered tree, the plaster cur脙漏, contemplating with amazement all these things from the past that she knew so well. How distant the ball already seemed to her! What was it that put such a distance between the morning of the day before yesterday and the evening of this day? Her trip to La Vaubyessard had made a hole in her life, like those great chasms that a storm, in a single night, will sometimes open in the mountains. Yet she resigned herself: reverently she put away in the chest of drawers her beautiful dress and even her satin shoes, whose soles had been yellowed by the slippery wax of the dance floor. Her heart was like them: contact with wealth had laid something over it that would not be wiped away.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other one contains serious mistranslations. For example, the French indirect object pronoun "lui" can mean either "him" or "her". The character at the center of this excerpt is clearly a woman. The subject pronoun "elle" (unambiguously feminine) is used throughout the text and all its instances are correctly translated into "she" in both translations. However, even though the bad translation uses "she" in the case of subject pronouns, when it comes to the object pronoun, it is translated as "him" in "How far off the ball already seemed to him!" Additionally, the possessive pronoun "son" (his/her) is also translated in the masculine three times, in "His trip to Vaubyessard had made a hole in his life" and in "His heart was like them" (in the bad translation). It is very strange that half the pronouns would be in the feminine and the other half in the masculine when clearly we're talking about one and the same person (a woman). This bad translation makes me think that it is a machine translation, and that the program does not take the context into account enough.

source: Je tiens d芒聙聶un vieux professeur (c芒聙聶est du plus loin qu芒聙聶il me souvienne) que Platon appelait la mati脙篓re l芒聙聶autre. C芒聙聶est 脙 la, b脙陋te qu芒聙聶il faut s芒聙聶en prendre, 脙 cet 脙陋tre sensible, parfaitement distinct de l芒聙聶脙垄me, v脙漏ritable individu, qui a son existence s脙漏par脙漏e, ses go脙禄ts, ses inclinations, sa volont脙漏, et qui n芒聙聶est au-dessus des autres animaux que parce qu芒聙聶il est mieux 脙漏lev脙漏 et pourvu d芒聙聶organes plus parfaits.
translation_a: An old professor once told me (that芒聙聶s as much as I can remember) that Plato called matter the other. That芒聙聶s quite good, yet I would rather give that quintessential name to the beast that is united with our soul. For that substance is the real other and pesters us in a most distressing manner. It is rather frequently observed that man is twofold, but this is, they say, because he is made up of a soul and a body; and this body is accused of I know not how many dreadful things, quite inappropriately I am sure, since it is as incapable of feeling as it is of thinking. The real culprit is the beast, that sentient being utterly distinct from the soul, that veritable individual that has his own separate existence, tastes, inclinations and will, and is superior to the other animals only because he happens to be a little more well-bred and endowed with more perfect organs.
translation_b: I learned from an old professor (it is as far back as I can remember) that Plato called matter the other. It is very good; but I would rather give this name par excellence to the beast which is joined to our soul. It is really this substance which is the other, and which lures us in such a strange way. We realize very roughly that man is double, but it is, they say, because he is composed of a soul and a body; and we accuse this body of I don't know how many things, but very badly certainly, since it is as incapable of feeling as of thinking. It is the beast that must be blamed, this sentient being, perfectly distinct from the soul, a true individual, which has its separate existence, its tastes, its inclinations, its will, and which does not is above other animals only because he is better brought up and provided with more perfect organs.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other one is too literal and doesn't sound like English at times. For example, in the French sentence "[...] il est aussi incapable de sentir que de penser [...]" there is a comparison between two infinitive verbs. In French, you don't need to repeat the conjugated verb "est" (='is'), but you do in English. The good translation got this right in "[...] it is as incapable of feeling as it is of thinking [...]" while the bad translation only uses one conjugated verb - which is what we have in French syntax but is not ok in English syntax: "[...] it is as incapable of feeling as of thinking [...]"

source: Un souci meilleur vint le distraire, 脙 savoir la grossesse de sa femme. 脙聙 mesure que le terme en approchait, il la ch脙漏rissait davantage. L芒聙聶id脙漏e d芒聙聶avoir engendr脙漏 le d脙漏lectait. Rien ne lui manquait 脙 pr脙漏sent. Il connaissait l芒聙聶existence humaine tout du long, et il s芒聙聶y attablait sur les deux coudes avec s脙漏r脙漏nit脙漏.
translation_a: A more agreeable concern came to distract him, namely, his wife芒聙聶s pregnancy. As her term drew near, she became all the more dear to him. Another bond of the flesh was being established between them, and something like a pervasive sense of a more complex union. When, from a distance, he watched her indolent steps and her waist turning gently above her uncorseted hips, when, across from her, he contemplated her at his ease, as she sat tired in her armchair, his happiness could no longer be contained; he would stand up, he would kiss her, run his hands over her face, call her 芒聙聹little mama,芒聙?try to get her to dance, and, half laughing, half crying, babble all sorts of fond pleasantries that came into his head. The idea of having engendered a child delighted him. Nothing was lacking to him now. He knew the entire scope of human existence, and he sat down to it serenely with both elbows on the table.
translation_b: A better concern came to distract him, namely his wife's pregnancy. As the term approached, he cherished her more. It was another bond of the flesh being established and like the continuing feeling of a more complex union. When he saw her lazy gait from afar and her waist turn limply on her hips without a corset, when opposite each other he gazed at her quite at ease and when she took, seated, poses tired in her chair, so her happiness was no longer contained; he got up, kissed her, passed his hands over her face, called her little mamma, wanted to make her dance, and spouted, half laughing, half crying, all sorts of caressing jokes that came to his mind. The idea of having fathered delighted him. Nothing was missing now. He knew human existence all along, and he propped himself up on both elbows with serenity.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is fully idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. For example, the following sentence from the bad translation would be difficult to understand for someone who is not given the French text as well: "[...] he gazed at her quite at ease and when she took, seated, poses tired in her chair [...]". source: La vie du bord devint l芒聙聶objet de s脙漏rieuses m脙漏ditations ; Hatteras la r脙漏gla avec le plus grand soin, et le r脙篓glement fut affich脙漏 dans la salle commune. Les hommes se levaient 脙 six heures du matin ; les hamacs 脙漏taient expos脙漏s 脙 l芒聙聶air trois fois par semaine ; le plancher des deux chambres fut frott脙漏 chaque matin avec du sable chaud ; le th脙漏 br脙禄lant figurait 脙 chaque repas, et la nourriture variait autant que possible suivant les jours de la semaine ; elle se composait de pain, de farine, de gras de b脜聯uf et de raisins secs pour les puddings, de sucre, de cacao, de th脙漏, de riz, de jus de citron, de viande conserv脙漏e, de b脜聯uf et de porc sal脙漏, de choux, et de l脙漏gumes au vinaigre ; la cuisine 脙漏tait situ脙漏e en dehors des salles communes ; on se privait ainsi de sa chaleur ; mais la cuisson des aliments est une source constante d芒聙聶脙漏vaporation et d芒聙聶humidit脙漏.
translation_a: Life on board became the subject of serious consideration; Hatteras regulated it with the greatest attention, the guidelines being posted in the common room. The men got up at six; the hammocks were aired three times a week; the floors of both rooms were scrubbed with hot sand each morning; boiling tea accompanied each meal; the food changed as much as possible, depending on the day of the week: sugar, cocoa, tea, rice, lemon juice, preserved meat, beef, salt pork, cabbage, and pickled vegetables; plus puddings made from bread, flour, beef suet, and raisins; the kitchen was situated outside the common rooms; its heat was lost, but cooking is a constant source of evaporation and hence humidity.
translation_b: Life on board became the object of serious meditation; Hatteras settled it with the greatest care, and the rules were posted in the common room. The men got up at six o'clock in the morning; the hammocks were exposed to the air three times a week; the floor of the two rooms was scrubbed every morning with hot sand; hot tea figured at every meal, and the food varied as much as possible according to the days of the week; it consisted of bread, flour, beef fat and raisins for puddings, sugar, cocoa, tea, rice, lemon juice, preserved meat, salted beef and pork, cabbage, and pickled vegetables; the kitchen was located outside the common rooms; they thus deprived themselves of their warmth; but cooking food is a constant source of evaporation and moisture.
comment: The translation I selected is much more idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other one is too literal and contains mistranslations. For example, the French verb "r脙漏gler" (line 1) has multiple meanings (to regulate, to settle, to adjust, to tune, etc.) The bad translation picked one of the meanings that is obviously not appropriate for the context. It picked the meaning of "settle" while the good translation picked the correct meaning of "regulate".

source: Le soir, pourtant, l芒聙聶abb脙漏 Mouret se sentit trop las. Il remit son d脙漏part au lendemain. Le lendemain, il se donna un nouveau pr脙漏texte : il ne pouvait abandonner sa s脜聯ur ainsi seule avec la Teuse ; il laisserait une lettre pour qu芒聙聶on la conduis脙庐t chez l芒聙聶oncle Pascal. Pendant trois jours, il se promit d芒聙聶脙漏crire cette lettre ; la feuille de papier, la plume et l芒聙聶encre 脙漏taient pr脙陋tes, sur la table, dans sa chambre. Et, le troisi脙篓me jour, il s芒聙聶en alla, sans 脙漏crire la lettre. Tout d芒聙聶un coup, il avait pris son chapeau, il 脙漏tait parti pour le Paradou, par b脙陋tise, obs脙漏d脙漏, se r脙漏signant, allant l脙 comme 脙 une corv脙漏e qu芒聙聶il ne savait de quelle fa脙搂on 脙漏viter. L芒聙聶image d芒聙聶Albine s芒聙聶脙漏tait encore effac脙漏e ; il ne la voyait plus, il ob脙漏issait 脙 d芒聙聶anciennes volont脙漏s, mortes en lui 脙 cette heure, mais dont la pouss脙漏e persistait dans le grand silence de son 脙陋tre.
translation_a: However, when evening came, Abb脙漏 Mouret felt too weary. He put off his departure to the next day. On the next day, he found another pretext: he could not leave his sister like that, on her own with La Teuse; he would leave a letter, asking for her to be taken to Uncle Pascal. For three days he promised himself he would write that letter; the sheet of paper, the pen and ink were ready on the table in his bedroom. And on the third day he went off without writing the letter. He had suddenly taken his hat and set off for the Paradou, out of stupidity, just obsessed and resigning himself to it, going there as if for some chore he could find no way of avoiding. The image of Albine had grown even fainter; he could not see her any more, he was acting in obedience to old resolves, now dead for him, but whose impetus still persisted in the great silence of his being.
translation_b: In the evening, however, Father Mouret felt too tired. He postponed his departure until the following day. The next day he gave himself a new pretext: he could not leave his sister thus alone with La Teuse; he would leave a letter to be taken to Uncle Pascal. For three days he promised himself to write this letter; the sheet of paper, the pen and the ink were ready, on the table, in his room. And on the third day he went away without writing the letter. All of a sudden, he had taken his hat, he had left for Paradou, out of stupidity, obsessed, resigning himself, going there like a chore he didn't know how to avoid. Albine's image had faded again; he no longer saw her, he was obeying old wills, dead within him at that moment, but whose thrust persisted in the great silence of his being.
comment: The translation I selected is better because it is much more idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. Also, the other one contains errors/mistranslations. For example, in the French sentence [...] il laisserait une lettre pour qu芒聙聶on la conduis脙庐t chez l芒聙聶oncle Pascal" the object pronoun "la" (="her" or "it" depending on whether the object is animate or inanimate) obviously refers to the sister mentioned earlier. The GOOD translation has this pronoun correctly referring to the sister: [...] he would leave a letter, asking for her to be taken to Uncle Pascal" where "her" is the sister. However, the BAD translation misunderstood the "la" and translated it as if it referred to the letter itself "[...] he would leave a letter to be taken to Uncle Pascal."

source: Au mois de mai, l芒聙聶attente du jeune pr脙陋tre 脙漏tait pleine d芒聙聶un heureux espoir. Il ne s芒聙聶inqui脙漏tait m脙陋me plus des gronderies de la Teuse. S芒聙聶il restait si tard 脙 prier dans l芒聙聶脙漏glise, c芒聙聶脙漏tait avec l芒聙聶id脙漏e folle que la grande Vierge dor脙漏e finirait par descendre. Elle 脙漏tait la M脙篓re de Dieu ; elle avait l芒聙聶ampleur f脙漏conde, la face auguste, les bras forts de l芒聙聶脙聣pouse divine portant J脙漏sus. Elle 脙漏tait la Vierge de ses jours de d脙漏faillance, la Vierge s脙漏v脙篓re qui lui rendait la paix int脙漏rieure par la redoutable vision du paradis.
translation_a: In the month of May, the expectation of the young priest was full of happy hope. He was no longer even bothered by La Teuse's scolding. If he stayed so late to pray in the church, it was with the mad idea that the great golden Virgin would end up descending. And yet, he dreaded her, this Virgin who looked like a princess. He didn't like all Virgins the same. This struck him with sovereign respect. She was the Mother of God; she had the fruitful fullness, the august face, the strong arms of the divine Spouse carrying Jesus. He imagined her thus in the midst of the celestial court, letting the tail of her royal cloak trail among the stars, too high for him, so powerful that it would crumble into powder, if she deigned to lower her eyes to his. She was the Virgin of her days of failure, the stern Virgin who restored her inner peace through the dreadful vision of paradise.
translation_b: In the month of May, the young priest芒聙聶s waiting was filled with a joyous hope. He didn芒聙聶t even worry any more about La Teuse芒聙聶s scoldings. If he stayed so late at prayer in the church it was with the mad idea that the tall gilded Virgin would finally step down to him. However, he also feared her, this Virgin who looked so like a princess. He did not love all Virgins in the same way. This one aroused in him a sovereign respect. She was the Mother of God; she had the fullness of fecundity, the august countenance, and the powerful arms of the Divine Spouse carrying Jesus. He imagined her in this way, in the courts of Heaven, letting the train of her royal robe float among the stars, too high for him, so mighty that he would crumble into dust if she deigned to lower her eyes to meet his. comment: The translation I did NOT select is way too literal. It keeps all the same word categories as French. Often, when you translate into another language you should use the same word but change its category (verb, noun, adjective, etc.) to make it flow better in the target language. For example, an English verb can be translated into French as a noun (e.g., She cried after Jean left -> Elle a pleur脙漏 apr脙篓s le d脙漏part de Jean (lit: after Jean's *departure* - Noun)). In this text, an example of that would be "S芒聙聶il restait si tard 脙 prier dans l芒聙聶脙漏glise" where PRIER (=to pray) is a verb. The GOOD translation changed the category and has it as: "If he stayed so late at prayer" where PRAYER is a noun. The bad translation kept it as a verb and thus it sounds too literal.

source: Quand l芒聙聶abb脙漏 Mouret se retrouva seul, dans la poussi脙篓re du chemin, il se sentit plus 脙 l芒聙聶aise.
