translation_a: When Father Mouret found himself alone, in the dust of the road, he felt more at ease. These stony fields gave back to his dream of harshness, of inner life lived in the desert. Along the sunken lane, the trees had dropped a disturbing coolness on his neck, which the blazing sun was now drying. The sparse almond trees, the poor wheat, the infirm vines, on both sides of the road, soothed him, drew him from the confusion into which the excessively greasy breaths of Paradou had thrown him. And, in the midst of the blinding light that streamed from the sky onto this bare earth, Jeanbernat's blasphemies no longer cast even a shadow. He was overjoyed when, raising his head, he saw on the horizon the motionless helm of the Solitaire, with the stain of the pink tiles of the church.
translation_b: When Abb脙漏 Mouret was alone again, walking in the dust of the road, he felt more at ease. These stony fields brought back his dreams of severity, of an inner life lived in a desert. All along the lane the trees had dripped splashes of disturbing coolness on his neck, which were now being dried by the burning sun. The lean almond trees, the meagre corn, and the sickly vines on each side of the road seemed to calm him, drawing him out from the troubled state into which the too rich atmosphere of the Paradou had thrown him. And in the blinding light that flowed down from the sky upon this barren ground, the blasphemies of Jeanbernat no longer cast the slightest shadow. He felt a sudden surge of joy when, raising his head, he saw in the distance the motionless outline of the Lone Tree, and the patch of pink tiles on the church.
comment: The translation I selected is more idiomatic and flows better in English. The one I did NOT select includes a couple of nonsensical sentences. For example, the French "Ces champs pierreux rendaient 脙 son r脙陋ve de rudesse [...]" is quite poetic and the intended meaning must be gained from outside the literal meaning. Unfortunately, the translation I did NOT select did not go beyond the literal meaning, and the English resulting sentence does not make sense: "These stony fields gave back to his dream of harshness [...]" - The translation I selected, on the other hand, found a way to convey the meaning and to make sense: "These stony fields brought back his dreams of severity [...]"

comment: The translation I selected is by far the best. It is very idiomatic and does not stick to French syntax. Also, the bad translation includes some serious errors. For example, at the beginning, it seems to suggest that the main character, Georges, is a woman. It says "Her eyes and her mind [...]" (meaning; Georges's eyes and mind). translation_a: However, on the 19th Simpson succeeded in seizing one of them four hundred yards from the ship; he had taken the precaution of plugging his hole of refuge, so that the animal was at the mercy of the hunters. He struggled for a long time, and after having suffered several shots, he was finally knocked out. It was nine feet long; its bull-dog head, the sixteen teeth of its jaws, its large pectoral fins in the shape of fins, its small tail provided with another pair of fins, made it a magnificent specimen of the family of sea dogs. The doctor, wanting to keep his head for his collection of natural history, and his skin for future needs, had both prepared quickly and inexpensively. He plunged the body of the animal into the fire pit, and thousands of small shrimps removed the smallest bits of flesh; at the end of half a day the work was accomplished, and the most skilful of the honorable corporation of tanners of Liverpool could not have succeeded better.
translation_b: Still, on the 19th, Simpson succeeded in getting one four hundred yards distant from the ship; he had taken the precaution to close its hole in the ice, so that it could not escape from its pursuers. He fought for a long time, and died only after receiving many bullets. He was nine feet long; his bull-dog head, the sixteen teeth in his jaw, his large pectoral fins shaped like little wings, his little tail with another pair of fins, made him an excellent specimen. The doctor wished to preserve his head for his collection of natural history, and his skin for future contingences, hence he prepared both by a rapid and economical process. He plunged the body in the hole, and thousands of little prawns removed the flesh in small pieces; at the end of half a day the work was half finished, and the most skilful of the honorable corporation of tanners at Liverpool could not have done better.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is much more idiomatic and sounds like it could be the original text. The bad translation sticks to the French too much, and does not flow in English.

translation_a: Emma did not answer. She was breathing hard and staring wildly all around, while the countrywoman, frightened by her face, was instinctively backing away, thinking she was mad. Suddenly she struck her forehead and cried out, for the memory of Rodolphe, like a great bolt of lightning on a dark night, had entered her mind. He was so good, so sensitive, so generous! And besides, if he should hesitate to do her this service, she would know quite well how to force him by reminding him, with a single glance, of their lost love. She therefore set off toward La Huchette, quite unaware that she was hastening to yield to the very thing that had, only recently, so enraged her, nor in the least suspecting that she was prostituting herself.
translation_b: Emma didn't answer. She was panting, rolling her eyes around her, while the peasant woman, frightened by her face, instinctively recoiled, thinking her mad. Suddenly she struck her forehead, uttered a cry, for the memory of Rodolphe, like a great flash of lightning in a dark night, had passed through her soul. He was so good, so delicate, so generous! And, moreover, if he hesitated to do her this favor, she would know how to compel him to do so by recalling their lost love with a single wink. So she set off for La Huchette, without noticing that she was running to offer herself to what had just so exasperated her, nor suspecting the least in the world of this prostitution.

For example, the French bit "[...] la croyant folle" does not need a conjugated verb in French. However, keeping it this way in English makes it sound weird. The translation I selected added a verb as in "[...] thinking she was mad" while the translation I did not select kept the French syntax "[...] thinking her mad".

translation_a: We were in the first days of October. There was fog over the countryside. Vapors stretched out on the horizon, between the outline of the hills; and others, tearing themselves apart, mounted, were lost. Sometimes, in the parting of the clouds, under a ray of sunshine, one could see in the distance the roofs of Yonville, with the gardens at the edge of the water, the courtyards, the walls, and the steeple of the church. The clumps of trees, here and there, jutted out like black rocks; and the high rows of poplars, which rose above the mist, represented shores stirred by the wind.
translation_b: It was the beginning of October. There was a haze over the countryside. Mist lay along the horizon, between the outlines of the hills; and elsewhere it tore apart, rose, vanished. Sometimes, through a gap in the haze, one could see the roofs of Yonville under a ray of sunlight in the distance, with its gardens by the water芒聙聶s edge, its courtyards, walls, and church steeple. translation_a: 芒聙聹I assure you,芒聙?said Aspasia, 芒聙聹that most of the head-dresses represented in the pages I have been turning over deserve the same fate as yours, so very extravagant are they.芒聙?The fair Athenian amused herself vastly in looking over the engravings, and was very reasonably surprised by the variety and oddity of modern contrivances. One figure, especially struck her. It was that of a young lady with a really elegant head-dress which Aspasia only thought somewhat too high. But the piece of gauze that covered the neck was so very full you could scarcely see half her face. Aspasia, not knowing that these extraordinary developments were produced by starch, could not help showing a surprise which would have been redoubled (but inversely), had the gauze been transparent.
translation_b: "I assure you," Aspasie told him, "that most of the hairstyles represented in the notebook I'm leafing through would deserve the same fate as yours, they are so extravagant!" The beautiful Athenian was extremely amused perusing these prints, and was justly amazed at the variety and oddity of the modern adjustments. One figure among others struck her: it was that of a young lady represented with the most elegant hairstyle, and whom Aspasie found only a little too high; but the piece of gauze that covered the throat was of such extraordinary size that 芒聙聹you could barely see half of the face芒聙? Aspasia, not knowing that these prodigious forms were only the work of starch, could not help showing an astonishment which would have redoubled in the opposite direction if the gauze had been transparent.
comment: This one was a difficult choice because there are oddities in both translations. For example, in the first translation, I find it strange that "(but inversely)" should be between parentheses. It would have been easier and more elegant to use the adverb "inversely" directly next to the verb. Also, the first translation sounds a little bit more literal, but not too literal. My issue with the second translation is that the author chose not to translate the name of the character (Aspasie) - which is fine - but towards the end the author translated the name into Aspasia (with an -a at the end). I don't have a preference for Aspasie over Aspasia. I think either one would be ok. But the lack of consistency within one text makes this translation look very bad, and the author look very careless.

source: Candide, dans le fond de son coeur, n芒聙聶avait aucune envie d芒聙聶脙漏pouser Cun脙漏gonde. Mais l芒聙聶impertinence extr脙陋me du baron le d脙漏terminait 脙 conclure le mariage, et Cun脙漏gonde le pressait si vivement qu芒聙聶il ne pouvait s芒聙聶en d脙漏dire. Il consulta Pangloss, Martin et le fid脙篓le Cacambo. Pangloss fit un beau m脙漏moire par lequel il prouvait que le baron n芒聙聶avait nul droit sur sa soeur, et qu芒聙聶elle pouvait, selon toutes les lois de l芒聙聶Empire, 脙漏pouser Candide de la main gauche. Martin conclut 脙 jeter le baron dans la mer. Cacambo d脙漏cida qu芒聙聶il fallait le rendre au levanti patron et le remettre aux gal脙篓res; apr脙篓s quoi on l芒聙聶enverrait 脙 Rome au p脙篓re g脙漏n脙漏ral par le premier vaisseau. L芒聙聶avis fut trouv脙漏 fort bon; la vieille l芒聙聶approuva; on n芒聙聶en dit rien 脙 sa soeur; la chose fut ex脙漏cut脙漏e pour quelque argent, et on eut le plaisir d芒聙聶attraper un j脙漏suite et de punir l芒聙聶orgueil d芒聙聶un baron allemand.
translation_a: Candide, in the bottom of his heart, had no desire to marry Cun脙漏gonde. But the baron's extreme impertinence determined him to conclude the marriage, and Cunegonde pressed him so earnestly that he could not back down. He consulted Pangloss, Martin, and the faithful Cacambo. Pangloss wrote a fine memoir by which he proved that the Baron had no right over his sister, and that she could, according to all the laws of the Empire, marry Candide with her left hand. Martin concluded to throw the baron into the sea. Cacambo decided that he had to be returned to the levanti boss and handed over to the galleys; after which he would be sent to Rome to the Father General by the first ship. The advice was found very good; the old woman approved of it; nothing is said to his sister; the thing was carried out for some money, and they had the pleasure of catching a Jesuit and punishing the pride of a German baron.
translation_b: Candide had in truth no real desire to marry Miss Cun脙漏gonde; but the Baron芒聙聶s extreme impertinence persuaded him to conclude the match; and Cun脙漏gonde pressed him so eagerly that he could not back out. He consulted Pangloss, Martin, and the faithful Cacambo. Pangloss composed a fine treatise, by which he proved that the baron had no right over his sister; and that she might, according to all the laws of the empire, marry Candide with the left hand.cp Martin thought they should throw the Baron into the sea; Cacambo decided that he must be delivered to the Turkish captain and sent to the galleys, and then they should send him by the first ship to the Father-General in Rome. This advice seemed to be very good: the old woman approved of it, and nothing was said to his sister. The business was executed at a small price; and they had the pleasure of tricking a Jesuit and punishing the pride of a German baron.
comment: The translation I selected is much more idiomatic and accurate. The one I did NOT select contains many errors. For example, in French, you can read "le levanti patron". The adjective "levanti" in French is EXTREMELY rare. Actually I had to look it up myself as I had never seen it before. It seems to be an archaic form for "Turkish soldier" that was only used by Voltaire (the author of this text). In the translation that I selected, the translator chose to sort of explain what "levanti" is by translating "le levanti patron" directly into "the Turkish captain". I think this is a good choice which helps the modern reader understand what's going on without having to look things up. Even if one thinks that keeping the archaic form is best, I highly doubt it would be "levanti boss" - there should at least be a capital L (Levanti boss/captain).

source: Il y a tant de personnes curieuses dans le monde ! 芒聙?Je suis persuad脙漏 qu芒聙聶on voudrait savoir pourquoi mon voyage autour de ma chambre a dur脙漏 quarante-deux jours au lieu de quarante-trois, ou de tout autre espace de temps ; mais comment l芒聙聶apprendrais-je au lecteur, puisque je l芒聙聶ignore moi-m脙陋me ? Tout ce que je puis assurer, c芒聙聶est que, si l芒聙聶ouvrage est trop long 脙 son gr脙漏, il n芒聙聶a pas d脙漏pendu de moi de le rendre plus court ; toute vanit脙漏 de voyageur 脙 part, je me serais content脙漏 d芒聙聶un chapitre. J芒聙聶脙漏tais, il est vrai dans ma chambre, avec tout le plaisir et l芒聙聶agr脙漏ment possibles ; mais, h脙漏las ! je n芒聙聶脙漏tais pas le ma脙庐tre d芒聙聶en sortir 脙 ma volont脙漏 ; je crois m脙陋me que sans l芒聙聶entremise de certaines personnes puissantes qui s芒聙聶int脙漏ressaient 脙 moi, et pour lesquelles ma reconnaissance n芒聙聶est pas 脙漏teinte, j芒聙聶aurais eu tout le temps de mettre un in-folio au jour, tant les protecteurs qui me faisaient voyager dans ma chambre 脙漏taient dispos脙漏s en ma faveur !
translation_a: There are so many curious people in the world! 芒聙聹I am sure people would like to know why my journey around my room lasted forty-two days instead of forty-three, or any other space of time; but how should I inform the reader of this, since I am ignorant of it myself? All I can assure is that, if the work is too long for his taste, it was not up to me to make it shorter; all traveler's vanity aside, I would have contented myself with a chapter. I was, it is true, in my room, with all possible pleasure and comfort; but unfortunately ! I was not free to leave it at my will; I even believe that without the intervention of certain powerful people who were interested in me, and for whom my gratitude is not extinguished, I would have had plenty of time to bring a folio to light, both the protectors who made me travel to my room were arranged in my favour!
translation_b: There are so many curious people in this world.芒聙聰I am convinced that some would like to know why my voyage around my room lasted forty-two days instead of forty-three or some other length of time. But how can I impart this to the reader, if I myself do not know? All I can say with any assurance is that if this work is too long for his liking, it was not within my power to shorten it: a traveler芒聙聶s vanity notwithstanding, I would have been content with a single chapter. I was, it is true, quite pleased and amused to stay in my room; but I was not, alas, free to come out at will. Indeed, I believe that without the intervention of certain powerful persons interested in my case, and for whom my gratitude is most keen, I should have had all the time I needed to bring out an in-folio volume, so disposed in my favor were the protectors who sent me on my voyage around my room.
comment: The translation I selected is much more idiomatic and accurate. The translation I did NOT select contains errors. For example, the French word "tant" was translated as "both" in the BAD translation. It is true that it can sometimes mean "both" but that is definitely not the case in this text.

source: Le pr脙陋tre 脙漏prouvait un malaise. Il se sentait sans force pour ramener 脙 Dieu cet 脙漏trange vieillard, dont la raison lui parut singuli脙篓rement d脙漏traqu脙漏e. Maintenant, il se rappelait certains bavardages de la Teuse sur le Philosophe, nom que les paysans des Artaud donnaient 脙 Jeanbernat. Des bouts d芒聙聶histoires scandaleuses tra脙庐naient vaguement dans sa m脙漏moire. Il se leva, faisant un signe au docteur, voulant quitter cette maison, o脙鹿 il croyait respirer une odeur de damnation. Mais, dans sa crainte sourde, une singuli脙篓re curiosit脙漏 l芒聙聶attardait. Il restait l脙 , allant au bout du petit jardin, fouillant le vestibule du regard, comme pour voir au del脙 , derri脙篓re les murs. Par la porte grande ouverte, il n芒聙聶apercevait que la cage noire de l芒聙聶escalier. Et il revenait, cherchant quelque trou, quelque 脙漏chapp脙漏e sur cette mer de feuilles, dont il sentait le voisinage, 脙 un large murmure qui semblait battre la maison d芒聙聶un bruit de vagues.
translation_a: The priest felt ill at ease. He didn芒聙聶t feel strong enough to bring back to God this strange old man, whose mind seemed to him to be strangely disordered. Now he began to recall some of La Teuse芒聙聶s gossipings about the Philosopher, as the peasants of Les Artaud called Jeanbernat. Vague scraps of scandalous stories lurked in his memory. He stood up, with a sign to the doctor that he wanted to leave this house in which he felt he was inhaling the odour of damnation. But despite his confused fearfulness, a strange curiosity held him back. He stayed on, going to the end of the little garden, intently scanning the hallway, as if to see beyond it, behind the walls. Through the wide open door, he could see only the dark of the staircase. And he went back again, looking for some gap, some little glimpse on to that sea of foliage that he knew to be very near from the great murmuring that seemed to beat against the house with a sound like breaking waves.
translation_b: The priest felt uneasy. He felt himself powerless to bring back to God this strange old man, whose reason seemed to him singularly unhinged. Now he remembered certain gossip from La Teuse about the Philosopher, the name which the peasants of Artaud gave to Jeanbernat. Bits of scandalous stories lingered vaguely in his memory. He got up, making a sign to the doctor, wanting to leave this house, where he thought he breathed in an odor of damnation. But, in his dull fear, a singular curiosity lingered on him. He remained there, going to the end of the little garden, searching the vestibule with his gaze, as if to see beyond, behind the walls. Through the wide open door, he could see only the black stairwell. And he would come back, looking for some hole, some escape on this sea of leaves, whose neighborhood he felt, by a great murmur which seemed to beat the house with the sound of waves.
comment: The translation I selected is much more idiomatic. The translation I did NOT select sticks to the French grammar too much and, from time to time, does not even make sense at all. For example, towards the end of the BAD translation: "[...] sea of leaves, whose neighborhood he felt" to mean that the leaves were close does not make sense. The translation I selected sort of paraphrased the idea and conveys something much more sensible: "[...] sea of foliage that he knew to be very near"

source: La mari脙漏e avait suppli脙漏 son p脙篓re qu芒聙聶on lui 脙漏pargn脙垄t les plaisanteries d芒聙聶usage. Cependant, un mareyeur de leurs cousins (qui m脙陋me avait apport脙漏, comme pr脙漏sent de noces, une paire de soles) commen脙搂ait 脙 souffler de l芒聙聶eau avec sa bouche par le trou de la serrure, quand le p脙篓re Rouault arriva juste 脙 temps pour l芒聙聶en emp脙陋cher, et lui expliqua que la position grave de son gendre ne permettait pas de telles inconvenances. Le cousin, toutefois, c脙漏da difficilement 脙 ces raisons. En dedans de lui-m脙陋me, il accusa le p脙篓re Rouault d芒聙聶脙陋tre fier, et il alla se joindre dans un coin 脙 quatre ou cinq autres des invit脙漏s qui, ayant eu par hasard plusieurs fois de suite 脙 table les bas morceaux des viandes, trouvaient aussi qu芒聙聶on les avait mal re脙搂us, chuchotaient sur le compte de leur h脙麓te et souhaitaient sa ruine 脙 mots couverts.
translation_a: The bride had begged her father to be spared the usual jokes. However, a fishmonger of their cousins (who had even brought a pair of soles as a wedding present) was beginning to blow water with his mouth through the keyhole, when Father Rouault arrived just in time to to prevent it, and explained to him that the serious position of his son-in-law did not allow such improprieties. The cousin, however, reluctantly yielded to these reasons. Inside himself, he accused old Rouault of being proud, and he went to join in a corner four or five other guests who, having by chance had several times in a row at the table the low cuts of meat, also thought that they had been badly received, whispered about their host and wished his ruin in veiled words.
translation_b: The bride had begged her father that she be spared the customary pranks. Nevertheless, one of their cousins, a fishmonger (who had actually brought, as a wedding present, a couple of soles), was about to squirt water with his mouth through the keyhole, when P脙篓re Rouault came along just in time to stop him, explaining that the importance of his son-in-law芒聙聶s position did not permit of such improprieties. The cousin, however, yielded only with difficulty to these arguments. Inwardly, he accused P脙篓re Rouault of being proud, and he went off into a corner to join four or five other guests who, having by chance been served the cheapest cuts of meat several times in succession at table, also felt they had been poorly treated and were whispering against their host, quietly hoping he would ruin himself.
comment: The translation I selected is much more idiomatic and does not stick too much to the French grammar and vocabulary. There are several misinterpretations in the translation that I did NOT select, but which were translated appropriately in the one I chose. Many of the lexical items in the French text are polysemous with the least frequent meaning being the one used here. The translation I selected correctly picked the least frequent meaning (as it was correct in this context) while the other translation just chose the most frequent meaning, which would be the meaning listed first in the dictionary. For example, the French verb "recevoir" usually means "to receive" / "to get". In this text, however, it clearly means "to host"/"to welcome"/"to treat" https://www.larousse.fr/dictionnaires/francais-anglais/recevoir/66229

source: L芒聙聶abb脙漏 Mouret d脙漏pensa l脙 ses 脙漏conomies du s脙漏minaire. C芒聙聶脙漏taient, d芒聙聶ailleurs, des embellissements dont la na脙炉vet脙漏 maladroite e脙禄t fait sourire. La ma脙搂onnerie le rebuta vite. Il se contenta de recr脙漏pir le tour de l芒聙聶脙漏glise, 脙 hauteur d芒聙聶homme. La Teuse g脙垄chait le pl脙垄tre. Quand elle parla de r脙漏parer aussi le presbyt脙篓re, qu芒聙聶elle craignait toujours, disait-elle, de voir tomber sur leurs t脙陋tes, il lui expliqua qu芒聙聶il ne saurait pas, qu芒聙聶il faudrait un ouvrier ; ce qui amena une querelle terrible entre eux. Elle criait qu芒聙聶il n芒聙聶脙漏tait pas raisonnable de faire si belle une 脙漏glise o脙鹿 personne ne couchait, lorsqu芒聙聶il y avait 脙 c脙麓t脙漏 des chambres dans lesquelles on les trouverait s脙禄rement morts, un de ces matins, 脙漏cras脙漏s par les plafonds.
translation_a: Abb脙漏 Mouret spent all his seminary savings on the work. His embellishments were so clumsy and naive as to raise a smile. The masonry-work soon lost its appeal for him. He contented himself with replastering all round the church to the height of a man芒聙聶s head. La Teuse mixed the plaster. When she spoke of repairing the presbytery too, which she still feared would fall down on their heads, he explained that he wouldn芒聙聶t know how to do that, it would need a builder; and this led to a terrible quarrel between the two. She yelled that it wasn芒聙聶t sensible so to beautify a church in which nobody slept, when right next door there were bedrooms in which they would surely some morning be found dead, crushed beneath the ceiling.
translation_b: Father Mouret spent his seminary savings there. They were, moreover, embellishments whose clumsy simplicity would have made you smile. Masonry soon put him off. He contented himself with replastering around the church, at eye level. La Teuse ruined the plaster. When she spoke of repairing the presbytery as well, which she said she was always afraid of seeing fall on their heads, he explained to her that he would not know, that a workman would be needed; which led to a terrible quarrel between them. She cried out that it was unreasonable to make a church so beautiful where no one slept, when there were rooms nearby in which they would surely be found dead, one of these mornings, crushed by the ceilings.
comment: The translation I selected is more idiomatic and sounds like it could be the original text. It also refers to elements from earlier in the text (e.g., The first line in French includes the adverb "l脙 " which means "there". In the translation I selected, "l脙 " is translated by "on the work" to mean that that is what the Abb脙漏 spent all his money on. It makes it easier to understand and the text flows better with such references). Also, I found a serious misinterpretation in the second text. In French, the verb "g脙垄cher" usually means "to waste" / "to ruin". However, when used with "pl脙垄tre" (=plaster), it means "to mix" / "to temper" -- This is a collocation that the author of the second translation missed (https://www.collinsdictionary.com/dictionary/french-english/g%C3%A2cher) but that was translated correctly in the passage I selected.

translation_a: Even at the beginning, as soon as the prince entered the drawing room, he sat down as far as possible from the Chinese vase, with which Aglaya had so frightened him. Is it possible to believe that after Aglaya's words yesterday, some indelible conviction came into him, some amazing and impossible premonition that he would certainly break this vase tomorrow, no matter how he avoided it, no matter how he avoided trouble? But it was. In the course of the evening other strong but bright impressions began to flood into his soul; we already talked about this. He forgot his premonition. When he heard about Pavlishchev and Ivan Fyodorovich let him down and showed him again to Ivan Petrovich, he moved closer to the table and fell straight into an armchair beside a huge, beautiful Chinese vase, which stood on a pedestal, almost next to his elbow, a little behind.
translation_b: From the very beginning, as soon as the prince entered the drawing room, he sat down as far as possible from the Chinese vase, with which Aglaya had frightened him so. Can one possibly believe that, after Aglaya芒聙聶s words the day before, some sort of indelible conviction settled in him, some sort of astonishing and impossible premonition that the next day he would unfailingly break that vase, however far away he kept from it, however much he avoided the disaster? But it was so. In the course of the evening other strong but bright impressions began to flow into his soul; we have already spoken of that. He forgot his premonition. When he heard about Pavlishchev, and Ivan Fyodorovich brought him and introduced him again to Ivan Petrovich, he moved closer to the table and ended up right in the armchair next to the enormous, beautiful Chinese vase, which stood on a pedestal almost at his elbow, slightly behind him.
comment: I prefer the second translation for the following reasons: 1) According to the source text the premonition can't be described as "amazing" as it is given in the first translation. 2) There is a redundancy in the phrase "no matter how he avoided it, no matter how he avoided trouble" in the first translation while the exact same phrase in the second translation sounds just fine: "however far away he kept from it, however much he avoided the disaster" 3) The verb "fell straight into an armchair" from the first translation doesn't convey the meaning of the phrase from the source text while "ended up right in the armchair" from the second translation does it properly

translation_a: But it turned out that you are just a dignitary, ignorant and stupid, like all the dignitaries who were before you and will be after you. And, like all dignitaries, you are a tyrant. Only tyranny can explain the hurricane you raised around a piece of mediocre sausage, or the fact that you brought an unknown young man from the street to your place. And, perhaps, out of the same tyranny, you brought Volodya Makarov closer to you, about whom I know only one thing, that he is a football player. You are a bard. You need jesters and freeloaders. I have no doubt that that Volodya Makarov ran away from you, unable to endure bullying. Like me, you must have systematically turned him into a fool.
translation_b: 芒聙聹But it turned out that you芒聙聶re simply a functionary, ignorant and dull, like all functionaries who were before you and will be after you. And like all functionaries you芒聙聶re a two-bit tyrant. Only by two-bit tyranny can the hurricane which you raised around a piece of mediocre sausage or the fact that you brought home an unknown young man off the street be explained. And perhaps out of the same two-bit tyranny you brought near to yourself Volodya Makarov, about whom I know only one thing, that he is a soccer player. You are a lord. You need jesters and hangers-on. I don芒聙聶t doubt that this Volodya Makarov ran away from you, not having endured the derision. It must be that just like me, you were systematically turning him into a fool.
comment: Both translations are almost equally perfect but the second one is still better. 1) "lord" from the second translation is the exact translation of the Russian "脨卤脨掳脩聙脨赂脨陆" while "bard" from the first translation doesn't convey a necessary meaning 2) According to the source text the last sentence implies the meaning of continuous action as it is important for the story. The author of the second translation is right to use Past Continuous

translation_a: As a soccer player Volodya represented a complete opposite to Getzke. Volodya was a professional sportsman芒聙聯the other was a professional player. Important to Volodya was the general course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome芒聙聯Getzke strove only to show his own skill. He was an old, experienced player, not intending to maintain the honor of the team: he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sort of sports organization because he had compromised himself by moves from club to club for money. They forbade him to participate in matches for the play-off of a championship. They invited him only for friendly games, demonstration matches and trips to other countries. Skill united in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He was suspicious of the players芒聙聯both those with whom he played and the opponents. He knew that he would drive in the balls on any team. The rest was not important to him. He was a hack.
translation_b: As a football player, Volodya was the exact opposite of Getska. Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player. Volodya was interested in the overall course of the game, the overall victory, the outcome - Getsuke sought only to show his art. He was an old, experienced player who did not intend to uphold the honor of the team; he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sports organization, because he compromised himself by moving from club to club for money. He was banned from participating in championship matches. He was invited only to a friendly game, exhibition matches and trips to other countries. Art was combined in him with luck. His participation made the team dangerous. He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents. He knew that he would score goals for any team. The rest didn't matter to him. He was a hack.
comment: There is no doubt that the first translation is better than the second one for the following reasons: 1) There is a mistake in spelling the name of the character in the second translation. It should be "Getzke" while the author of the second translation writes "Getska" or even "Getsuke". 2) According to the source text a story is told about two characters. The second sentence of the passage should give information about two different people while there is only one person mentioned in the second sentence of the 2nd translation: "Volodya was a professional athlete - he was a professional player" 3) The author of the second translation uses a word-by-word translation of the sentence "He despised the players - and those with whom he played, and opponents" which cannot be used here

translation_a: This is what I can't figure out. After all, no matter how limited their minds, they still had to understand that such a life was a real massacre - only slowly, day after day. The state (humanity) forbade killing one to death and did not forbid killing millions by half. To kill one, that is, to reduce the sum of human lives by 50 years, is criminal, but to reduce the sum of human lives by 50 million years is not criminal. Well, isn't it funny? With us, this mathematical-moral problem can be solved in half a minute by any ten-year-old number; they could not - all their Kants together (because none of the Kants guessed to build a system of scientific ethics, that is, based on subtraction, addition, division, multiplication).
translation_b: Try as I may, I cannot understand it. After all, no matter how limited their intelligence, they should have understood that such a way of life was truly mass murder芒聙聰even if slow murder. The state (humaneness) forbade the killing of a single individual, but not the partial killing of millions day by day. To kill one individual, that is, to diminish the total sum of human lives by fifty years, was criminal. But to diminish the sum of human lives by fifty million years was not considered criminal. Isn't that absurd? Today, any ten-year-old will solve this mathematical-moral problem in half a minute. They, with all their Kants taken together, could not solve it (because it never occurred to any of the Kants to build a system of scientific ethics, i.e., ethics based on subtraction, addition, division, and multiplication).
comment: The second translation is better for the following reasons: 1) The author of the first translation translated the phrase "脨聺脩聝, 脩聙脨掳脨路脨虏脨碌 脨陆脨碌 脩聛脨录脨碌脩聢脨陆脨戮?" as "Well, isn't it funny?" which is word-by-word translation. It is necessary to convey the mood of the narrator and his attitude to the problem he describes. That's why the phrase "Isn't that absurd?" from the second translation sounds much better. 2) The same problem with the second translation - word-by-word translation of the phrase "脨拢 脨陆脨掳脩聛 脩聧脩聜脩聝 脨录脨掳脩聜脨碌脨录脨掳脩聜脨赂脩聡脨碌脩聛脨潞脨赂-脨录脨戮脩聙脨掳脨禄脩聦脨陆脩聝脩聨 脨路脨掳脨麓脨掳脩聡脩聝 脨虏 脨驴脨戮脨禄脨录脨赂脨陆脩聝脩聜脩聥 脩聙脨碌脩聢脨赂脩聜 脨禄脩聨脨卤脨戮脨鹿 脨麓脨碌脩聛脩聫脩聜脨赂脨禄脨碌脩聜脨陆脨赂脨鹿 脨陆脩聝脨录脨碌脩聙" - "With us, this mathematical-moral problem can be solved in half a minute by any ten-year-old number" which makes absolutely no sense. translation_a: He was accompanied, first, by Lebedev芒聙聶s nephew, already known to the reader, and, second, by Ippolit. Ippolit was a very young man, about seventeen, or perhaps eighteen, with an intelligent but constantly irritated expression on his face, on which illness had left its terrible marks. He was thin as a skeleton, pale yellow, his eyes glittered, and two red spots burned on his cheeks. He coughed incessantly; his every word, almost every breath, was accompanied by wheezing. A rather advanced stage of consumption was evident. It seemed that he had no more than two or three weeks left to live. He was very tired, and sank into a chair before anyone else. The rest made some show of ceremony on entering and were all but abashed, though they looked grave and were obviously afraid of somehow losing their dignity, which was strangely out of harmony with their reputation as negators of all useless social trivialities, prejudices, and almost everything in the world except their own interests.
translation_b: He was accompanied, firstly, by Lebedev's nephew, known to readers, and secondly, by Ippolit. Hippolyte was a very young man, about seventeen, maybe eighteen, with an intelligent, but constantly irritated expression on his face, on which illness left terrible marks. He was thin as a skeleton, pale yellow, his eyes sparkled, and two red spots burned on his cheeks. He coughed incessantly; his every word, almost every breath, was accompanied by a wheeze. Consumption was visible in a very strong degree. It seemed that he had no more than two or three weeks to live. He was very tired and sank into a chair before everyone else. The rest, at the entrance, were somewhat ceremonious and almost embarrassed, looked, however, solemnly and apparently afraid to somehow lose dignity, which strangely did not harmonize with their reputation as deniers of all useless secular trifles, prejudices, and almost everything in the world, except for their own interests. .
comment: I consider the first translation to be better than the second one. Besides an accurate vocabulary of the first translation it's necessary to pay attention to the next details: 1) There is a mistake in the second translation - its author changes the spelling of one character's name: "Ippolit" appears in the first sentence and is followed by "Hippolyte" in the second sentence. This Russian name cannot change its form. 2) In order to clarify the consequences of the illness Past Perfect should be used in the sentence "intelligent but constantly irritated expression on his face, on which illness had left its terrible marks". The author of the second translation doesn't use Past Perfect

translation_a: Since he often jumped from one thing to another and forgot what he had begun to say, the prince kept still so as to let him speak everything out. But all the same it was extremely unclear whether the letters had gone through him or through Vera. If he himself insisted that 芒聙聹to Rogozhin was all the same as to Nastasya Filippovna,芒聙?it meant that most likely they had not gone through him, if there were any letters at all. And how it happened that the letter now ended up with him, remained decidedly unexplained; most likely of all would be to suppose that he had somehow stolen it from Vera 芒聙?carried it off on the sly and taken it with some sort of intention to Lizaveta Prokofyevna. So the prince finally figured it out and understood it.
translation_b: Since he often strayed from one to the other and forgot what he began to talk about, the prince fell silent to let him speak. But all the same, it was extremely unclear: did the letters go through him or through Vera? If he himself assured that "to Rogozhin is the same as to Nastasya Filippovna," then it means, rather, that they did not go through him, if only there were letters. The incident, how the letter now came to him, remained decidedly unexplained; most likely it was necessary to assume that he somehow stole it from Vera ... quietly stole it and took it with some intention to Lizaveta Prokofievna. So the prince thought and understood at last.
comment: The first translation is a better one for various reasons: Grammar: according to the source text Past perfect should be used to identify a series of actions that happened before other actions in the past. Past Perfect is used in the first translation Vocabulary: "And how it happened that the letter now ended up with him" in the first translation is more accurate and conveys the meaning better than "The incident, how the letter now came to him" in the second translation. The same thing with "carried it off on the sly" (1st translation) over "quietly stole it". Moreover, there is a tautology in the second translation: "he somehow stole it from Vera ... quietly stole it and took it". The last sentence in the second translation isn't accurate and doesn't sum up the idea presented in the passage, which makes the sentence in the first translation better.

translation_a: The Christian teaching given to me in childhood disappeared in me as in others, only with the difference that as I began to read a great deal and to think very early on, so my rejection of Christian teaching became a conscious one very early on. From the age of sixteen I stopped saying my prayers and of my own volition stopped going to church and fasting. I stopped believing in all that had been given to me since childhood, but I did believe in something. What I did believe in I couldn芒聙聶t possibly have said. I did believe in God or rather did not deny God, but I couldn芒聙聶t have said what kind of god; I didn芒聙聶t deny Christ and his teaching, but I also couldn芒聙聶t have said where his teaching lay.
translation_b: The doctrine communicated to me from childhood disappeared in me just as it did in others, with the only difference being that since I began to read and think a lot very early, my renunciation of the doctrine became conscious very early. From the age of sixteen, I stopped standing up for prayer and stopped, on my own impulse, going to church and fasting. I stopped believing in what I was told from childhood, but I believed in something. What I believed in, I could never say. I also believed in God, or rather I did not deny God, but which God I could not say; I did not deny Christ and his teaching, but what his teaching was, I could not say either.
comment: I consider the first translation to be more accurate when it comes to certain details: 1) The source text makes it clear that the narrator tells about Christian teaching, that is why I don't see any reason to call it just "doctrine" as in the second translation 2) The phrase "of my own volition" in the first translation conveys the meaning of the source sentence much better than "on my own impulse" in the second translation 3) According to the source text the narrator "couldn芒聙聶t have said what kind of god" he believed, that is why the translation "which God I could not say" in the second translation is inappropriate 4) If we consider the context of the source text I believe it's better to use Past Perfect in the sentences that refer to the prior actions in the past. Past Perfect is used in the first translation

translation_a: This unexpected blow nearly killed my father. He lost his usual firmness, and his grief (usually mute) poured out in bitter complaints. "How!" he repeated, losing his temper. - 芒聙聹My son participated in the plans of Pugachev! Good God, what have I come to! The empress saves him from execution! Does that make it easier for me? The execution is not terrible: my ancestor died at the place of execution, defending what he considered the shrine of his conscience; my father suffered along with Volynsky and Khrushchev. But the nobleman should change his oath, unite with robbers, murderers, runaway lackeys!.. Shame and disgrace to our family! human opinion. My father was inconsolable.
translation_b: This unexpected blow very nearly killed my father. He lost his habitual self-control, and his grief, usually silent, found expression in bitter complaints. 芒聙聹What!芒聙?he repeated, beside himself. 芒聙聹My son is an accomplice of Pugachov芒聙聶s! Merciful heavens, what have I lived to see! The Empress reprieves him! Does that make it any better for me? It芒聙聶s not the death penalty that is terrible. My great-grandfather died on the scaffold for what was to him a matter of conscience; my father suffered, together with Volynsky and Khrushchov.a But for a gentleman to betray his oath of allegiance and join brigands, murderers and runaway serfs! Shame and disgrace to our name!芒聙?Terrified by his despair, my mother did not dare to weep in his presence and tried to cheer him by talking of the uncertainty of rumor and the small faith to be attached to people芒聙聶s opinions. My father was inconsolable.
comment: The second translation provides a more accurate translation of the phrases from the source text that cannot be translated word by word. The author of the second translation has managed to perfectly convey the meaning of the sentences "But for a gentleman to betray his oath of allegiance and join brigands, murderers and runaway serfs" (which is translated in a wrong way in the first translation and thus failing to convey the meaning of the Russian sentence) and "Terrified by his despair, my mother did not dare to weep in his presence and tried to cheer him by talking of the uncertainty of rumor and the small faith to be attached to people芒聙聶s opinions" (which is missing in the first translation).

translation_a: Empty. Quiet. The wind is there, behind the walls, as far away as that day when, shoulder to shoulder, two-one, we came out from below, from the corridors - if only it really was. I walked under some kind of stone arches, where the steps, hitting the damp arches, fell behind me - as if all the time another was walking behind me on my heels. Yellow - with red brick spots - the walls followed me through the dark square glasses of the windows, watched me open the melodious doors of barns, as I peered into corners, dead ends, nooks and crannies. The gate in the fence and the wasteland - a monument to the Great Bicentennial War: from the earth - bare stone ribs, yellow grinning jaws of the walls, an ancient furnace with a vertical pipe - forever a petrified ship among stone yellow and red brick bursts.
translation_b: Empty. Quiet. Wind outside, behind the walls, distant as the day when, shoulder to shoulder, two as one, we came out from below, from the corridors 芒聙聰if, indeed, this ever really happened. I walked beneath stone archways where my steps, resounding from the damp vaults, seemed to fall behind me, as if someone followed on my heels. Yellow walls with scars of red brick watched me through the dark glass squares of their windows, watched me open the singing doors of barns, peer into corners, dead ends, nooks, and crannies. A gate in the fence, and a desolate vacant lot芒聙聰memorial of the great Two Hundred Years' War. Rising from the earth-bare stony ribs, the yellow grinning jaws of walls, an ancient stove with a vertical chimney芒聙聰a ship forever petrified among the stony splashes of red and yellow brick.
comment: The second translation conveys the poetic component of the passage much more accurately. The author of the second translation seems to have been focused on the word choice more than the meaning these words convey. It can be seen in the phrase "Yellow - with red brick spots - the walls" which doesn't correspond to the meaning of the phrase in the source text while the same phrase in the second translation is just fine. The same thing with "as far away as that day" and "two-one" sound better in the second translation as "distant as the day" and "two as one".

translation_a: The news of my arrest was a shock to my family. Marya Ivanovna had told my parents of my strange acquaintance with Pugachov so simply that, so far from being troubled about it, they often laughed at it with whole-hearted amusement. My father refused to believe that I could have been implicated in vile rebellion the aim of which was to overthrow the throne and exterminate the gentry. He closely questioned Savelyich. The old man did not conceal the fact that I had been to see Pugachov and that the villain had been kind to me; but he swore that he had not heard of any treason. My parents were reassured and waited impatiently for favorable news. Marya Ivanovna was very much alarmed but said nothing, for she was extremely modest and prudent.
translation_b: The news of my arrest shocked my entire family. Marya Ivanovna told my parents so simply about my strange acquaintance with Pugachev that it not only did not disturb them, but also made them often laugh from the bottom of their hearts. Batiushka did not want to believe that I could be involved in a vile rebellion, the purpose of which was the overthrow of the throne and the extermination of the noble family. He severely interrogated Savelich. The uncle did not hide the fact that the master was visiting Emelka Pugachev, and that the villain still complained about something; but he swore that he had never heard of any betrayal. The old people calmed down and began to look forward to favorable news. Marya Ivanovna was greatly alarmed, but kept silent, for she was gifted to the highest degree with modesty and caution.
comment: The first translation is more accurate. Let's have a look at the grammar. The source text makes it clear that Past Perfect should be used to identify the sequence of actions in the past: "The old man did not conceal the fact that I had been to see Pugachov and that the villain had been kind to me" but he swore that he had not heard of any treason" and it is used in the first translation. Moreover, the sentence mentioned above is incorrectly translated in the second translation, which makes it impossible to understand the whole passage. Vocabulary. The second translation doesn't provide an accurate translation of several words. It should be "My father" (as in the first translation) but not "Batiushka", "The old man" but not "The uncle", "My parents" but not "The old people "

translation_a: In the evening he sits at home blanketed by the palm tree green of a lamp shade. Before him are sheets of paper, notebooks, and little slips with columns of figures. He throws back pages of the desk calendar, jumps up, checks the book rack, takes out some packets, and with his belly on the table, supporting his fat face with his hands, reads. The green field of the table is covered with a sheet of glass. So what is so special about that? A man is working, a man is at home in the evening working. A man staring at a piece of paper is twisting a pencil in his ear. Nothing special. But his whole behavior says: you are a Philistine, Kavalerov. Of course, he doesn芒聙聶t state this. There musn芒聙聶t even be anything of the sort on his mind. But it芒聙聶s clear without words. Some third person announces it to me. Some third person forces me to fly into a fit at the very time I芒聙聶m watching him.
translation_b: In the evening, at home, he sits, shaded by the palm greens of the lampshade. In front of him are sheets of paper, notebooks, small sheets of paper with columns of numbers. He flips the pages of the desk calendar, jumps up, looks in the bookcase, takes out packs, kneels on a chair and, with his stomach on the table, propping his fat face in his hands, reads. The green area of the table is covered with a glass plate. After all, what is special? A man works, a man is at home, in the evening, he works. The man, staring at the sheet, picks his ear with a pencil. Nothing special. But all his behavior says: you are an inhabitant, Kavalerov. Of course, he does not state this. There must be nothing like it in his mind. But it is clear without words. Someone else tells me about it. Someone else makes me mad while I'm watching him.
comment: Both translations are rather good when it comes to conveying the style of the source text. (even though "Philistine" from the first translation is a better equivalent to the Russian term "脨戮脨卤脩聥脨虏脨掳脩聜脨碌脨禄脩聦"), that is why I paid attention to the grammar and vocabulary. The first translation is way better. According to the source text, the actions are happening simultaneously ("A man staring at a piece of paper is twisting a pencil in his ear") which requires Present Continuous that is used in the first translation. Speaking of vocabulary, the phrases in the first translation ("Philistine", "third person forces me to fly into a fit") are more accurate and convey the exact meaning of the Russian sentences

translation_a: But Ganya had already endured too much that day and that evening, and was not prepared for this last unexpected trial. The crowd parted into two halves before him, and he was left face to face with Nastasya Filippovna, three steps away from her. She stood right by the fireplace and waited, not tearing her burning, intent gaze from him. Ganya, in a tailcoat, his hat and gloves in his hand, stood silent and unresponding before her, his arms crossed, looking at the fire. An insane smile wandered over his face, which was pale as a sheet. True, he could not take his eyes off the fire, off the smoldering packet; but it seemed something new had arisen in his soul; it was as if he had sworn to endure the torture; he did not budge from the spot; in a few moments it became clear to everyone that he would not go after the packet, that he did not want to.
translation_b: But Ganya had already endured too much that day and that evening, and he was not prepared for this last unexpected test. The crowd parted before them into two halves, and he remained eye to eye with Nastasya Filippovna, three paces away from her. She stood right by the fireplace and waited, not taking her eyes off him, fiery, intently. Ganya, in a tailcoat, with a hat in his hand and with gloves, stood in front of her silently and without answer, arms folded and looking at the fire. An insane smile roamed across his face as pale as a handkerchief. True, he could not take his eyes off the fire, from the smoldering pack; but it seemed that something new had entered his soul; as if he had sworn to endure torture; he didn't move; after a few moments it became clear to everyone that he would not go for a pack, he did not want to go.
comment: The grammar and style in both translations are just fine. That is why I prefer the first translation due to its vocabulary. Such words as "trial", "three steps away from her", "pale as a sheet" are the exact accurate equivalents to the ones used in the source text. There is still one tiny mistake in the first translation: "The crowd parted into two halves before him" should be replaced with "The crowd parted into two halves before them" and then the whole translation will be 100% perfect

translation_a: He did not finish; Ferdyshchenko quickly put a chair behind him, and the general, somewhat weak on his feet that afternoon, flopped down, or rather, fell on the chair, but this, however, did not embarrass him. He sat down directly opposite Nastasya Filippovna and with a pleasant grin, slowly and effectively, raised her fingers to his lips. In general, it was rather difficult to embarrass the general. His outward appearance, besides some slovenliness, was still quite decent, which he himself knew very well. He had happened before to be in very good society, from which he was finally excluded only two or three years ago. From the same period, he indulged too much without restraint in some of his weaknesses; but the dexterous and pleasant manner remained in him hitherto. Nastasya Filippovna seemed to be overjoyed at the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich, whom, of course, she knew from hearsay.
translation_b: He did not finish. Ferdyshchenko quickly offered him a chair from behind, and the general, somewhat weak in the legs after dinner, simply flopped or, better to say, collapsed into it; however, that did not embarrass him. He sat directly facing Nastasya Filippovna and, with a pleasant little grimace, slowly and dramatically brought her fingers to his lips. On the whole, it was rather difficult to embarrass the general. His appearance, apart from a certain slovenliness, was still quite decent, as he knew very well himself. In the past he had occasionally been received in very good society, from which he had been definitively excluded only two or three years ago. It was then that he gave himself over all too unrestrainedly to some of his weaknesses; but he still retained his adroit and pleasant manner. Nastasya Filippovna, it seemed, was exceedingly delighted by the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich, of whom she knew, of course, by hearsay.
comment: Both translations are quite good, but I prefer the second translation to the first one. The vocabulary in the second translation is more accurate: "weak in the legs" over "weak on his feet", "brought her fingers to his lips" over "raised her fingers to his lips", "was exceedingly delighted by the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich" over "seemed to be overjoyed at the appearance". Such small details lead to the understanding of the whole text.

translation_a: Lebedev drew himself up. It is true that he was sometimes even too naive and importunate in his curiosity; but at the same time he was a rather cunning and sinuous person, and in some cases even too insidiously silent; By continuous repulsions, the prince almost prepared an enemy for himself in him. But the prince repulsed him not because he despised him, but because the topic of his curiosity was delicate. Several days ago, the prince looked at some of his dreams as a crime, but Lukyan Timofeich took the prince芒聙聶s refusals for nothing more than personal disgust and distrust towards himself, went away with a wounded heart and was jealous of the prince, not only Kolya and Keller, but even his own daughter. , Vera Lukyanovna. Even at that very moment, perhaps, he could and wished to sincerely inform the prince of one piece of news that was extremely interesting for the prince, but he gloomily fell silent and did not tell him.
translation_b: Lebedev assumed a dignified air. It is true that he was sometimes even too na脙炉ve and importunate in his curiosity; but at the same time he was a rather cunning and devious man, and on certain occasions even too insidiously taciturn; by constantly rebuffing him, the prince had almost prepared in him an enemy for himself. But the prince rebuffed him not because he despised him, but because the theme of his curiosity was a delicate one. Only a few days ago the prince had looked upon some of his dreams as upon a crime, but Lukyan Timofeich had taken the prince芒聙聶s retorts as personal revulsion and suspicion towards himself, had gone away with a wounded heart, and was jealous not only of Kolya and Keller, but even of his own daughter, Vera Lukyanovna. Even at that very moment, he could have informed the prince of a certain piece of news interesting for him in the highest degree, and may have sincerely wished to, but he fell gloomily silent and did not inform him.
comment: The second translation is better for several reasons. The phrase "was jealous not only of Kolya and Keller, but even of his own daughter, Vera Lukyanovna" in the second translation is the exact accurate translation of the sentence in the source text while the phrase "was jealous of the prince, not only Kolya and Keller, but even his own daughter. , Vera Lukyanovna" contains a mistake that results in the misunderstanding of the meaning of the sentence as a whole. The sentence "he could have informed the prince of a certain piece of news interesting for him in the highest degree, and may have sincerely wished to" in the second translation not only is natural but also is grammatically correct while "he could and wished to sincerely inform the prince of one piece of news" isn't at all good enough to use.

translation_a: The prince finally began to feel not so much sorry as a bit ashamed. The thought even flashed in him: 芒聙聹Wouldn芒聙聶t it be possible to make something of this man under someone芒聙聶s good influence?芒聙?His own influence, for certain reasons, he considered quite unsuitable芒聙聰not out of self-belittlement, but owing to a certain special view of things. They gradually warmed to the conversation, so much so that they did not want to part. Keller confessed with extraordinary readiness to having done such things that it was impossible to imagine how one could tell about them. Starting out each time, he would positively insist that he was repentant and inwardly 芒聙聹filled with tears,芒聙?and yet he would tell of his action as if he were proud of it, and at the same time occasionally in such a funny way that he and the prince would end up laughing like crazy.
translation_b: The prince finally felt not exactly sorry, but as if ashamed. The thought even flashed through his mind: "Couldn't something be made out of this man by someone's good influence?" He considered his own influence, for some reason, very worthless, not from self-deprecation, but from a certain special view of things. Little by little they began to talk, and to the point that they did not want to disperse. Keller confessed to such cases with unusual readiness that it was impossible to imagine how one could talk about such cases. Starting each story, he assured positively that he was repenting and inwardly 芒聙聹full of tears,芒聙?and meanwhile he told it as if he was proud of the deed, and at the same time it was sometimes so funny that he and the prince finally laughed like crazy.
comment: Both translations are good, but I like the first one more as it is more accurate when it comes to word choice: "but owing to a certain special view of things" over "but from a certain special view of things", "They gradually warmed to the conversation" in the first translation has the exact same meaning as the source text while "Little by little they began to talk" in the second translation is rather word-by-word translation. I also think that " Keller confessed with extraordinary readiness to having done such things" in the first translation brings more context that is necessary in comparison with "Keller confessed to such cases" in the second translation

translation_a: 芒聙聹Yes, a scoundrel, but one who should have been paid,芒聙?continued the young man. - And that he is a bastard, so I will testify, and not only because he beat you. This, prince, is one rejected officer, a retired lieutenant from the former Rogozhin company, and he teaches boxing. All of them are now wandering, as Rogozhin dispersed them. But what is worst of all is that I knew about him that he was a scoundrel, a scoundrel and a thief, and yet I sat down to play with him, and that, as I played the last ruble (we played sticks), I thought to myself: If I lose, I'll go to Uncle Lukyan, I'll bow - he won't refuse. This is meanness, this is so meanness! This is deliberate meanness!
translation_b: 芒聙聹Yes, to a blackguard, but who still had to be paid,芒聙?the young man went on. 芒聙聹And that he芒聙聶s a blackguard, I, too, will testify, not only because he gave you a beating. He芒聙聶s a rejected officer, Prince, a retired lieutenant from Rogozhin芒聙聶s former band, who teaches boxing. They芒聙聶re all wandering about now, since Rogozhin scattered them. But the worst thing is that I knew he was a blackguard, a scoundrel, and a petty thief, and I still sat down to play with him, and that, as I bet my last rouble (we were playing cribbage), I thought to myself: I芒聙聶ll lose, go to Uncle Lukyan, bow to him芒聙聰he won芒聙聶t refuse. That was meanness, that was real meanness!
comment: The second translation is better. The style and the vocabulary used are similar to the source text. Plus, it is better to use a possessive case ( "Rogozhin芒聙聶s") as in the second translation. I also prefer the phrase "I bet my last rouble" in the second translation to "I played the last ruble" as it is more accurate.

translation_a: Several times I was ready to fling the package over the railing. But I had only to picture to myself how, freeing itself from the wrapper in flight, the ill-fated piece of sausage falls and with the effect of a torpedo disappears into the waves芒聙聯when instantly another picture would set me to trembling. Coming down on me I saw Babichev, a thundering, invincible idiot with bulging eyes. I芒聙聶m afraid of him. He crushes me. He doesn芒聙聶t look at me芒聙聯but sees right through me. He doesn芒聙聶t look at me. Only from the side do I see his eyes; when his face is turned in my direction his gaze isn芒聙聶t there: only the pince-nez sparkles, two blind, round plates. It芒聙聶s not interesting for him to look at me, no time, no desire, but I understand that he sees right through me.
translation_b: Several times I was ready to throw the bundle over the railing. But as soon as I imagine how, freeing myself from the wrapper on the fly, the unfortunate piece of sausage falls and disappears into the waves with the effect of a torpedo, how instantly another idea threw me into a shiver. I saw Babichev approaching me, a formidable, irresistible idol with bulging eyes. I'm afraid of him. He presses me. He does not look at me - and sees through. He doesn't look at me. Only from the side I see his eyes, when his face is turned in my direction, there is no look from him: only pince-nez sparkles, two round blind plaques. He is not interested in looking at me, there is no time, no desire, but I understand that he sees right through me.
comment: There are mistakes in both translations: in the first one - the word "idol" should be used instead of "idiot". The reflexive pronoun "myself" in the second translation is used in the wrong place which influences the understanding of the sentence - there is simply no sense if we compare it to the source text. That is why I've picked the first translation as a better one. Moreover, I find the grammar pattern "Only from the side do I see his eyes" from the first translation more suitable. I also prefer the word "plates" in the first translation over "plaques" in this very context

translation_a: But now I allow myself to say that this was a cruel demand, at the same time I did not even think of it, I was only inexpressibly hurt. I was no longer in the position in which I was in my youth, thinking that everything in life is clear; After all, I came to faith because, besides faith, I found nothing, probably nothing, except death, so it was impossible to cast aside this faith, and I submitted. And I found in my soul a feeling that helped me bear it. It was a feeling of self-humiliation and humility. I resigned myself, swallowed this blood and body without blasphemous feeling, with a desire to believe, but the blow had already been dealt. And, knowing in advance what awaited me, I could no longer go another time.
translation_b: But now I let myself say it was a brutal requirement; then I didn芒聙聶t even think that, it was just inexpressibly painful for me. I was no longer in the situation I had been in my younger days, thinking that everything in life was clear; I had come to faith because apart from faith I had found nothing, really nothing but annihilation, so I couldn芒聙聶t reject this faith and I submitted. And I found a feeling in my soul that helped me to bear it. This was a feeling of self-abasement and humility. I humbled myself; I swallowed this flesh and blood without any feeling of blasphemy, with the desire to believe, but the blow had been struck. And knowing in advance what was waiting for me, I could no longer go a second time.
comment: The style of the source text is better conveyed in the second translation. The vocabulary in the second translation is better and more accurate. There is no tautology in the second translation as it is in the first translation ("self-humiliation and humility"). Grammar patterns are more natural in the second translation

translation_a: After these words Aglaya naturally set out at once after them all, as she had intended to do in any event. Prince Shch., who was sitting with Adelaida, at her request immediately agreed to accompany the ladies. Still earlier, at the beginning of his acquaintance with the Epanchins, he had been extremely interested when he heard about the prince from them. It turned out that he was acquainted with him, that they had become acquainted not long ago and had lived together for a couple of weeks in the same little town. That was about three months ago. Prince Shch. had even told them a good deal about the prince and generally spoke of him with great sympathy, so that now it was with genuine pleasure that he went to visit his old acquaintance. General Ivan Fyodorovich was not at home at the time. Evgeny Pavlovich also had not arrived yet.
translation_b: After these words, Aglaya, of course, immediately set off after everyone, which, however, she intended to do without it. Prince Sh., who was sitting with Adelaide, at her request, immediately agreed to accompany the ladies. Even before, at the beginning of his acquaintance with the Yepanchins, he became extremely interested when he heard from them about the prince. It turned out that he knew him, that they met somewhere recently and lived together for two weeks in some town. It was about three months ago. Prince Shch. even talked a lot about the prince and generally spoke of him very sympathetically, so that now he went to visit an old acquaintance with sincere pleasure. General Ivan Fedorovich was not at home this time. Yevgeny Pavlovich hasn't arrived yet either.
comment: The first translation is the best choice. The meaning of the first sentence of the text is better conveyed in the first translation while there is a mistake in the second translation in choosing the phrase "she intended to do without it". It is also necessary to use Past Perfect in the sentence "It turned out that he was acquainted with him, that they had become acquainted not long ago and had lived together for a couple of weeks in the same little town" and it is used in the first translation. And the whole passage in Russian refers to the past while the present perfect tense suddenly appears in the last sentence of the second translation which is a mistake.

translation_a: 芒聙聹And what then? I saw gracious, simple-hearted, intelligent people; I saw an old man who was gentle and heard out a boy like me; I see people capable of understanding and forgiveness, people who are Russian and kind, people almost as kind and cordial as I met there, almost no worse. You can judge how joyfully surprised I was! Oh, allow me to speak this out! I had heard a lot and believed very much myself that in society everything is a manner, everything is a decrepit form, while the essence is exhausted; but I can see for myself now that among us that cannot be; anywhere else, but not among us. Can it be that you are all now Jesuits and swindlers? I heard Prince N. tell a story tonight: wasn芒聙聶t it all artless, inspired humor, wasn芒聙聶t it genuinely good-natured? Can such words come from the lips of a 芒聙?dead man, with a dried-up heart and talent? Could dead people have treated me the way you have treated me?
translation_b: - So what? I saw graceful, simple-hearted, intelligent people; I saw an old man who caresses and listens to the boy, like me; I see people who are able to understand and forgive, Russian and kind people, almost as kind and warm-hearted as I met there, almost no worse. Judge how joyfully I was surprised! Oh, let me say it! I heard a lot and I myself very much believed that in the world everything is a manner, everything is a decrepit form, and the essence has dried up; but I myself now see that this cannot be with us; it is somewhere, but not here. Are you all now Jesuits and deceivers? I heard Prince N. say just now: Isn't this simple-hearted, inspired humor, isn't this true good nature? Can such words come out of the mouth of a man... dead, with a withered heart and talent? Could the dead have treated me like you did?
comment: The first translation is better. Speaking of grammar Past Perfect should be used as in the first translation: "I had heard a lot and believed very much...". The first translation conveys the meaning properly by using accurate vocabulary: "among us that cannot be; anywhere else, but not among us" over "this cannot be with us; it is somewhere, but not here", "I heard Prince N. tell a story tonight" is way better than "I heard Prince N. say just now", "lips" should be used as a better equivalent to the Russian word, but not "mouth" (the second translation)

translation_a: The stairs, on which the prince ran up from under the gate, led to the corridors of the first and second floors, along which the hotel rooms were located. This staircase, as in all houses built long ago, was of stone, dark, narrow, and wound around a thick stone pillar. On the first running platform in this column there was a recess, like a niche, no more than one step wide and half a step deep. A man, however, could fit here. Dark as it was, but, running up to the platform, the prince immediately discerned that here, in this niche, a man was hiding for some reason. The prince suddenly felt like passing by and not looking to the right. He took one step, but could not stand it and turned around.
translation_b: The stairs which the prince ran up from under the gateway led to the corridors of the first and second floors, on which the hotel rooms were located. This stairway, as in all houses built long ago, was of stone, dark, narrow, and winding around a thick stone pillar. On the first landing, this pillar turned out to have a depression in it, like a niche, no more than one pace wide and a half-pace deep. There was, however, room enough for a man. Having run up to the landing, the prince, despite the darkness, made out at once that a man was for some reason hiding there, in that niche. The prince suddenly wanted to walk past and not look to the right. He had already gone one step, but could not help himself and turned.
comment: Grammar is fine in both translations but I prefer the second one as the meaning is conveyed very precisely and close to the meaning of the Russian text. It is seen if we compare the sentence from the second translation "Having run up to the landing, the prince, despite the darkness, made out at once that a man was for some reason hiding there, in that niche" with a perfect vocabulary and structure to the sentence "Dark as it was, but, running up to the platform, the prince immediately discerned that here, in this niche, a man was hiding for some reason" from the first translation, which sounds machine. Vocabulary in the second translation is once again better: "landing" over "running platform", "could not help himself and turned" over "could not stand it and turned around".

translation_a: The prince, however, heard how he was called an idiot, and shuddered, but not because he was called an idiot. "Idiot" he immediately forgot. But in the crowd, not far from the place where he was sitting, from somewhere on the side - he would not have indicated exactly in what place and at what point - one face flashed, a pale face, with curly dark hair, with acquaintances, very familiar smile and look, flashed and disappeared. It could very well be that it was only his imagination; from the whole vision, he was impressed by the crooked smile, eyes and light green dandy neck tie that the gentleman flashed by. Whether this gentleman disappeared into the crowd or slipped into the station, the prince could not determine either.
translation_b: The prince, however, heard that he had been called an idiot, and gave a start, but not because he had been called an idiot. The 芒聙聹idiot芒聙?he forgot at once. But in the crowd, not far from where he was sitting, somewhere to the side芒聙聰he would not have been able to show in what precise place and in what spot芒聙聰a face flashed, a pale face with dark, curly hair, with a familiar, a very familiar, smile and gaze芒聙聰flashed and disappeared. It might well have been that he only imagined it; of the whole apparition he was left with the impression of the crooked smile, the eyes, and the pale green, foppish tie that the gentleman who flashed was wearing. Whether this gentleman disappeared in the crowd or slipped into the vauxhall, the prince also could not have determined.
comment: It is clear from the Russian text that Past Perfect is to be used, which we can see in the second translation. There is a necessary remark in the second translation: "foppish tie that the gentleman who flashed was wearing". "he would not have indicated exactly" from the first translation doesn't convey the meaning of the Russian sentence as it does the phrase "he would not have been able to show" from the second translation. I also like the word choice in the second translation more than in the first one.

translation_a: Then I felt it芒聙聰first at the back of my head, then on my left ear芒聙聰the warm, gentle breath of the guardian angel. He obviously noticed that the book on my knees was already closed and my thoughts were far away. No matter, I was prepared right then to peel open the pages of my brain before him: this was a calm, gratifying feeling. I remember: I even glanced around and I insistently and pleadingly looked him in the eye, but he didn芒聙聶t understand芒聙?or didn芒聙聶t want to understand 芒聙聰he didn芒聙聶t ask me about a thing. It was left to me to do one thing: tell everything to you, my unknown readers (now you are as dear to me, and as close and yet unattainable to me as he was in that moment).
translation_b: Then I again felt - first on the back of my head, then on my left ear - the warm, gentle breath of the guardian angel. He clearly noticed that the book on my lap was already closed and my thoughts were far away. Well, at least now I am ready to unfold the pages of my brain in front of him: it is such a calm, gratifying feeling. I remember: I even looked around, I persistently, pleadingly looked into his eyes, but he did not understand - or did not want to understand - he did not ask me anything ... I have only one thing left: to tell you everything, my unknown readers (now you are so for me the same roads, and close, and inaccessible - as he was at that moment).
comment: in the third sentence, the first translation conveys the exact same meaning as the Russian text. In contrast, the second one offers a word-by-word translation (sounds even like a machine translation) and therefore changes the idea of the sentence. The last phrase of the text sounds more natural and understandable in the first translation and will be even clear for non-native speakers. Even though the second translation isn't at all bad (vocabulary sometimes is even better than in the first translation) I consider the meaning to be more important than the word choice
